disproportionately impacted community to determine 
the capture and recovery or control requirements for 
the calendar year beginning in 2026. 
II.G.1.d.(ii)(E) 
An owner or operator must comply with 
any capture, recovery, or control requirement that 
becomes applicable solely due to a change in location 
as assessed pursuant to Section II.G.1.d.(ii)(D) by no 
later than July 1, 2026. 
II.G.1.d.(iii) 
Well liquids unloading events are not included in the 
calculation for purposes of Section II.G.1.d.(ii) where the 
need for well liquids unloading resulted from the infiltration of 
excess water directly caused by a nearby hydraulic 
fracturing event provided that the owner of the well to be 
unloaded provides the Division with at least 48 hours written 
notice (or as soon as possible prior to conducting well liquids 
unloading if 48 hours’ notice would require an alternative or 
extended well liquids unloading practice that increases 
emissions) of the intent to begin unloading and the unloading 
activities are completed within thirty (30) days of 
commencement of those activities. The notice must include 
an identification of the operator that conducted the fracturing 
event suspected of contributing to the infiltration of water and 
the well API number(s) of the well that was fractured. 
II.G.2. Recordkeeping 
II.G.2.a. 
Through January 31, 2020, the owner or operator must keep 
records of the cause, date, time, and duration of venting events 
under Section II.G. Records must be kept for two (2) years and 
made available to the Division upon request. 
II.G.2.b. 
Beginning January 31, 2020, or the date specified in Section 
II.G.2.b.(iii), the owner or operator must keep the following records 
for two (2) years and make records available to the Division upon 
request. 
II.G.2.b.(i) 
The cause of emissions (i.e., downhole well 
maintenance, well liquids unloading, well plugging), date, 
time, and duration of emissions under Section II.G.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
106 
II.G.2.b.(ii) 
The best management practices used to minimize 
hydrocarbon emissions or the safety needs that prevented 
the use of best management practices. 
II.G.2.b.(iii) 
Beginning July 1, 2020, the emissions associated with 
well liquids unloading, downhole well maintenance, and well 
plugging. 
II.G.2.c. 
Beginning January 1, 2023, in addition to the records in 
Section II.G.2.b., the owner or operator must keep the following 
records for five (5) years and make records available to the Division 
upon request. 
II.G.2.c.(i) 
The volume of gas vented during each downhole well 
maintenance, well liquids unloading and well swabbing, and 
well plugging event. 
II.G.2.c.(ii) 
The type of artificial lift used to reduce emissions 
pursuant to Section II.G.1.c.(vi); the number of well liquids 
unloading and well swabbing events resulting in emissions to 
atmosphere; or, if applicable, documentation of the 
justification for not having artificial lift under Section 
II.G.1.c.(vi). If plunger lift is installed, the number of cycles of 
the plunger. 
II.G.2.c.(iii) 
Whether the well liquids unloading or well swabbing 
event was controlled pursuant to Section II.G.1.d. and, if not, 
the justification for the exemption under Sections II.G.1.d.(i) 
or II.G.1.d.(ii), including all records relating to Section 
II.G.1.d.(iii) and records of production during the 30-day time 
period covered by Section II.G.1.d.(iii) and an estimate of the 
VOC and methane emissions during that same 30-day time 
period associated with the well liquids unloading or well 
maintenance activities. 
II.G.3. Reporting 
II.G.3.a. 
The owner or operator must submit a single annual report 
using a Division-approved format on or before June 30th of each 
year (beginning June 30th, 2021) that includes the following 
information regarding each downhole well maintenance, well liquids 
unloading, and well plugging event conducted the previous 
calendar year that resulted in emissions. 
II.G.3.a.(i) 
The API number of the well and the AIRS number of 
any associated storage tanks.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
107 
II.G.3.a.(ii) 
Whether the emissions occurred due to downhole well 
maintenance, well liquids unloading, well swabbing, or well 
plugging. 
II.G.3.a.(iii) 
The date, time, and duration of the downhole well 
maintenance, well liquids unloading, or well plugging event, 
and, beginning with the annual report for calendar year 2023 
whether the event was controlled. 
II.G.3.a.(iv) The best management practices used to minimize 
emissions, including the method used pursuant to Section 
II.G.1.c.(vi) beginning January 1, 2023. 
II.G.3.a.(v) 
Safety needs that prevented the use of best 
management practices to minimize emissions, if applicable. 
II.G.3.a.(vi) An estimate of the volume of natural gas, VOC, NOx, 
N2O, CO2, CO, ethane, and methane emitted from the well 
associated with well liquid unloading activities, downhole 
well maintenance, and well plugging event and the emission 
factor or calculation methodology used to determine the 
volume of natural gas and emissions. 
II.G.3.a.(vii) Beginning with the annual report submitted June 30th 
of 2023 (for calendar year 2022), whether the well identified 
in Section II.G.3.a.(i) is equipped with artificial lift. 
II.H. 
(State Only) Emission reductions from midstream segment pigging operations 
and blowdowns of piping and equipment. 
II.H.1. Pigging operations and blowdowns of piping and equipment located at 
natural gas compressor stations and natural gas processing plants. 
II.H.1.a. 
Consistent with the schedule for compliance in Section 
II.H.1.c., at natural gas compressor stations and natural gas 
processing plants in disproportionately impacted communities, 
midstream segment owners or operators must capture and recover 
hydrocarbon emissions from 
II.H.1.a.(i) 
Pigging units attached to a high-pressure pigging 
pipeline with an outside diameter of twelve (12) inches or 
greater. 
II.H.1.a.(ii) 
Pigging units with annual uncontrolled actual 
emissions equal to or greater than 0.5 tpy VOC or 1 tpy 
methane on a rolling 12-month basis, consistent with a 
Division-accepted method of calculation.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
108 
II.H.1.a.(iii) 
Blowdowns of compressors, where total uncontrolled 
actual blowdown emissions from all compressors are greater 
than or equal to 0.75 tpy VOC or 1.5 tpy methane on a 
rolling 12-month basis, consistent with a Division-accepted 
method of calculation. Hydrocarbons emitted during a 
compressor blowdown event where the physical volume of 
the compressor is less than fifty (50) cubic feet (cf) are not 
included in the emissions calculated for purposes of 
applicability of this Section II.H.1.a.(iii), provided the owner 
or operator maintains records of the dates and number of 
such events. 
II.H.1.a.(iv) 
Blowdowns of all equipment and piping not covered 
by Sections II.H.1.a.(i) through II.H.1.a.(iii) where the 
physical volume between isolation valves is greater than or 
equal to fifty (50) cf. This requirement does not apply if the 
owner or operator can demonstrate that the aggregate 
uncontrolled actual emissions from blowdowns of all 
equipment and piping subject to this Section II.H.1.a.(iv) are 
less than 0.75 tpy VOC and 1.5 tpy methane, provided the 
owner or operator maintains records of the dates and 
number of all blowdowns including blowdowns where the 
physical volume between isolation valves is greater than one 
(1) cf but less than fifty (50) cf. 
II.H.1.b. 
Consistent with the schedule for compliance in Section 
II.H.1.c., at all natural gas compressor stations and natural gas 
processing plants not located in a disproportionately impacted 
community, midstream segment owners or operators must capture 
and recover hydrocarbon emissions from 
II.H.1.b.(i) 
Pigging units attached to high-pressure pigging 
pipelines with an outside diameter of twelve (12) inches or 
greater. 
II.H.1.b.(ii) 
Pigging units with annual uncontrolled actual 
emissions equal to or greater than 1 tpy VOC or 2 tpy 
methane on a rolling 12-month basis, consistent with a 
Division-accepted method of calculation.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
109 
II.H.1.b.(iii) 
Blowdowns of compressors, where total uncontrolled 
actual blowdown emissions from all compressors are greater 
than or equal to 1 tpy VOC or 2 tpy methane on a rolling 12-
month basis, consistent with a Division-accepted method of 
calculation. Hydrocarbons emitted during a compressor 
blowdown event where the physical volume of the 
compressor is less than fifty (50) cf are not included in the 
emissions calculated for purposes of applicability of this 
Section II.H.1.b.(iii), provided the owner or operator 
maintains records of the dates and number of such events. 
II.H.1.b.(iv) 
Blowdowns of equipment and piping not covered by 
Sections II.H.1.b.(i) through II.H.1.b.(iii) where the physical 
volume between isolation valves is greater than or equal to 
fifty (50) cf. This requirement does not apply if the owner or 
operator can demonstrate that the aggregate uncontrolled 
actual emissions from blowdowns of all equipment and 
piping subject to this Section II.H.1.a.(iv) are less than 1 tpy 
VOC and 2 tpy methane, provided the owner or operator 
maintains records of the dates and number of all blowdowns 
including blowdowns where the physical volume between 
isolation valves is greater than one (1) cf but less than fifty 
(50) cf. 
II.H.1.c. 
Schedule for compliance with Sections II.H.1.a. and II.H.1.b. 
Midstream segment owners or operators must be in compliance 
II.H.1.c.(i) 
Upon commencement of operation for any natural gas 
compressor station or natural gas processing plant that 
commences operation on or after February 14, 2022. 
II.H.1.c.(ii) 
By January 1, 2023, at no less than fifty percent 
(50%) of natural gas compressor stations and natural gas 
processing plants that commenced operation before 
February 14, 2022, and that are located within a 
disproportionately impacted community. 
II.H.1.c.(iii) 
By June 1, 2023, at all natural gas compressor 
stations and natural gas processing plants that commenced 
operation before February 14, 2022, and that are located 
within a disproportionately impacted community. 
II.H.1.c.(iv) 
By January 1, 2024, for all natural gas compressor 
stations and natural gas processing plants that commenced 
operation before February 14, 2022.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
110 
II.H.1.c.(v) 
Within sixty (60) days of the first day of the month 
after which a pigging unit in a disproportionately impacted 
community not subject to Sections II.H.1.a.(i) or (ii) increases 
hydrocarbon emissions to 0.5 tpy VOC or 1 tpy methane 
after the applicable compliance date in Sections II.H.1.c.(i) 
through II.H.1.c.(iv), on a rolling twelve-month basis. 
II.H.1.c.(vi) 
Within sixty (60) days of the first day of the month 
after which a pigging unit not located in a disproportionately 
impacted community and not subject to Sections II.H.1.b.(i) 
or II.H.1.b.(ii) that increases hydrocarbon emissions to 1 tpy 
VOC or 2 tpy methane after the applicable compliance date 
in Sections II.H.1.c.(i) through II.H.1.c.(iv), on a rolling 
twelve-month basis. 
II.H.1.c.(vii) Within sixty (60) days of the first day of the month 
after which blowdowns of compressors or other equipment 
and piping with a physical volume of the compressor or 
between isolation valves of equal to or greater than 50 cf 
located at a natural gas compressor station or natural gas 
processing plant located in a disproportionately impacted 
community not subject to Sections II.H.1.a.(iii) or II.H.1.a.(iv) 
increases hydrocarbon emissions to 0.75 tpy VOC or 1.5 tpy 
methane after the applicable compliance date in Section 
II.H.1.c.(i)-(iv), on a rolling twelve-month basis. 
II.H.1.c.(viii) Within sixty (60) days of the first day of the month 
after which blowdowns of compressors or other equipment 
and piping with a physical volume of the compressor or 
between isolation valves of equal to or greater than 50 cf 
located at a natural gas compressor station or natural gas 
processing plant not located in a disproportionately impacted 
community not subject to Sections II.H.1.b.(iii) or II.H.1.b.(iv) 
increases hydrocarbon emissions to 1 tpy VOC or 2 tpy 
methane after the applicable compliance date in Sections 
II.H.1.c.(i) through II.H.1.c.(iv), on a rolling twelve-month 
basis.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
111 
II.H.1.c.(ix) 
An owner or operator may request an extension of the 
compliance schedules in Sections II.H.1.c.(ii) through 
II.H.1.c.(iv) for no more than twelve (12) months. The 
Division may approve such request if the owner or operator 
demonstrates that the extension is required to facilitate 
coordinated engineering and design projects to holistically 
address compliance with Section II.H. in order to avoid 
temporary solutions and emissions disbenefits, if any, that 
may be caused by the compliance schedules in Sections 
II.H.1.c.(ii) through II.H.1.c.(iv). 
II.H.1.c.(x) 
Beginning April 14, 2025, disproportionately impacted 
community is defined as in Section II.A.11.b. The owner or 
operator must assess by December 31, 2025, whether the 
natural gas compressor station or natural gas processing 
plant is located within a disproportionately impacted 
community to determine the capture and recovery or control 
requirements for the calendar year beginning in 2026. 
II.H.1.c.(xi) 
An owner or operator must comply with any capture, 
recovery, or control requirement that becomes applicable 
solely due to a change in location as assessed pursuant to 
Section II.H.1.c.(x) by no later than July 1, 2026. 
II.H.1.d. 
Midstream owners or operators must capture and recover 
hydrocarbon emissions from pigging units that commence 
operation after February 14, 2022, where the pigging unit is 
attached to a high-pressure pigging line. 
II.H.2. Pigging operations at standalone pigging stations. 
II.H.2.a. 
Midstream segment owners or operators must capture and 
recover hydrocarbon emissions from the following pigging 
operations at standalone pigging stations that commence operation 
on or after February 14, 2022. 
II.H.2.a.(i) 
Pigging units attached to a high-pressure pigging 
pipeline. 
II.H.2.a.(ii) 
Pigging units located in a disproportionately impacted 
community with annual uncontrolled actual emissions equal 
to or greater than 0.5 tpy VOC or 1 tpy methane on a rolling 
12-month basis, consistent with a Division-accepted method 
of calculation.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
112 
II.H.2.a.(iii) 
Pigging units not located in a disproportionately 
impacted community with annual uncontrolled actual 
emissions greater than or equal to 1 tpy VOC or 2 tpy 
methane on a rolling 12-month basis, consistent with a 
Division-accepted method of calculation. 
II.H.2.a.(iv) 
Beginning April 14, 2025, disproportionately impacted 
community is defined as in Section II.A.11.b. The owner or 
operator must assess by December 31, 2025, whether the 
pigging station is located within a disproportionately 
impacted community to determine the capture and recovery 
or control requirements for the calendar year beginning in 
2026. 
II.H.2.a.(v) 
An owner or operator must comply with any capture, 
recovery, or control requirement that becomes applicable 
solely due to a change in location as assessed pursuant to 
Section II.H.2.a.(iv) by no later than July 1, 2026. 
II.H.2.b. 
Beginning January 1, 2023, at standalone pigging stations 
that commenced operation before February 14, 2022, located 
within a disproportionately impacted community, midstream 
segment owners or operators must capture and recover 
hydrocarbon emissions from pigging operations 
II.H.2.b.(i) 
At pigging units with annual uncontrolled actual 
emissions equal to or greater than 0.5 tpy VOC or 1 tpy 
methane on a rolling 12-month basis, consistent with a 
Division-accepted method of calculation. 
II.H.2.b.(ii) 
Where the pigging unit is attached to a high-pressure 
pigging pipeline with an outside diameter of twelve (12) 
inches or greater. 
II.H.2.b.(iii) 
A pigging unit not subject to Section II.H.2.b.(i) as of 
January 1, 2023, that increases hydrocarbon emissions to 
0.5 tpy VOC or 1 tpy methane must be in compliance with 
Section II.H.2.b, within sixty (60) days of the first day of the 
month after which the emissions exceeded the applicable 
threshold, based on a rolling twelve-month basis.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
113 
II.H.2.b.(iv) 
Beginning April 14, 2025, disproportionately impacted 
community is defined as in Section II.A.11.b. The owner or 
operator must assess by December 31, 2025, whether the 
pigging station is located within a disproportionately 
impacted community to determine the capture and recovery 
or control requirements for the calendar year beginning in 
2026. 
II.H.2.b.(v) 
An owner or operator must comply with any capture, 
recovery, or control requirement that becomes applicable 
solely due to a change in location as assessed pursuant to 
Section II.H.2.b.(iv) by no later than July 1, 2026. 
II.H.2.c. 
Beginning January 1, 2024, at standalone pigging stations 
that commenced operation before February 14, 2022, that are not 
in a disproportionately impacted community, midstream segment 
owners or operators must capture and recover hydrocarbon 
emissions from pigging operations 
II.H.2.c.(i) 
At pigging units with annual uncontrolled actual 
emissions equal to or greater than 1 tpy VOC or 2 tpy 
methane on a rolling 12-month basis, consistent with a 
Division-accepted method of calculation. 
II.H.2.c.(ii) 
Where the pigging unit is attached to a high-pressure 
pigging pipeline with an outside diameter of twelve (12) 
inches or greater. 
II.H.2.c.(iii) 
A pigging unit not subject to Section II.H.2.c.(i) as of 
January 1, 2024, that increases hydrocarbon emissions to 1 
tpy VOC or 2 tpy methane must be in compliance with 
Section II.H.2.c. within sixty (60) days of the first day of the 
month after which the emissions exceeded the applicable 
threshold, based on a rolling twelve-month basis. 
II.H.2.c.(iv) 
Beginning April 14, 2025, disproportionately impacted 
community is defined as in Section II.A.11.b. The owner or 
operator must assess by December 31, 2025, whether the 
pigging station is located within a disproportionately 
impacted community to determine the capture and recovery 
or control requirements for the calendar year beginning in 
2026. 
II.H.2.c.(v) 
An owner or operator must comply with any capture, 
recovery, or control requirement that becomes applicable 
solely due to a change in location as assessed pursuant to 
Section II.H.2.c.(iv) by no later than July 1, 2026.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
114 
II.H.3. Capture and recovery requirements. 
II.H.3.a. 
Capture and recovery requirements apply during normal 
operation. 
II.H.3.b. 
Capture and recovery requirements do not apply during 
planned emergency system shutdown testing operations. 
II.H.3.c. 
Capture and recovery is not required pursuant to Sections 
II.H.1.a.(iv) or II.H.1.b.(iv) for blowdowns of storage vessels; 
pressure vessels; or process vessels such as surge vessels, 
bottom receivers, or knockout vessels, that operate at a pressure 
less than twenty (20) psig. 
II.H.3.d. 
Residual emission from depressurization of the blowdown 
volume remaining after capture and recovery techniques have been 
implemented are considered in compliance with the capture and 
recovery requirements of Sections II.H.1. and II.H.2. 
II.H.3.e. 
Where a natural gas compressor station or natural gas 
processing plant is connected to an electrical grid, capture and 
recovery techniques must be powered by non-emitting equipment, 
where technically and economically feasible. If technically or 
economically infeasible, the midstream owner or operator will 
maintain a record of the analysis undertaken at the time the pigging 
unit or piping and equipment became subject to Section II.H.1. 
II.H.3.f. 
If capture and recovery of the hydrocarbon emissions 
emitted is not feasible, the owner or operator may request Division 
approval to use a control device to comply with Sections II.H.1. or 
II.H.2. The Division may approve the use of open flares to control 
hydrocarbon emissions from pigging operations and blowdowns 
under Sections II.H.1. or II.H.2. Any Division approval will include 
appropriate operating and maintenance requirements for the control 
device utilized. 
II.H.3.f.(i) 
Pigging operations and blowdowns that are minimized 
through the use of a control device or closed-vent system as 
of February 14, 2022, or for which a permit application is 
pending to require the use of a control device or closed-vent 
system, as of December 31, 2021, do not need further 
Division approval to continue use of the control device or 
closed-vent system for purposes of Sections II.H.1. or II.H.2. 
The owner or operator utilizing control devices under this 
Section II.H.3.f.(i) must notify the Division by March 31, 
2022, that control devices will be used to comply with 
Sections II.H.1. or II.H.2.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
115 
II.H.3.g. 
Midstream owners or operators must design and operate 
natural gas compressor stations, natural gas processing plants, and 
standalone pigging stations that commence operation on or after 
January 1, 2023, to maximize the capture and recovery of 
hydrocarbon emissions from pigging operations and equipment and 
piping routinely blown down based on technologies and capabilities 
that are technically and economically feasible at the time of facility 
development. Midstream owners or operators must maintain a 
record of the analysis undertaken at the time of facility development 
pursuant to this section for the life of the facility. 
II.H.4. Beginning January 1, 2023, midstream segment owners or operators must 
utilize best practices to minimize emissions from pigging operations and 
blowdowns during normal operations, including all stand-alone pigging 
stations and midstream pipelines not located within the boundaries of a 
natural gas compressor station or natural gas processing plant, including 
II.H.4.a. 
Keeping pipeline access openings to the atmosphere on the 
pig receiver closed at all times except when a pig is being placed 
into or removed from the receiver or during active pipeline 
maintenance activities. 
II.H.4.b. 
In the 8-hour ozone control area and northern Weld County, 
utilizing a liquids management system to reduce the accumulation 
of liquids in the pigging unit. A liquids management system to 
include, but is not limited to, use of a pig ramp, process drain, pig 
receiver on an incline, or a closed liquids containment system. 
II.H.4.c. 
Where feasible for pipeline blowdowns other than for pigging 
operations, rerouting gas to the low-pressure system using existing 
piping connections between high- and low-pressure systems, 
temporarily resetting or bypassing pressure regulators to reduce 
system pressure prior to maintenance, or installing temporary 
connections between high- and low-pressure systems. 
II.H.4.c.(i) 
For purposes of Section II.H.4.c., feasibility requires 
that a low-pressure line be nearby, be owned or operated by 
the same midstream owner or operator, and be on 
contiguous property owned or operated by the midstream 
owner or operator. Feasibility here also means that the 
action is economically feasible. 
II.H.4.c.(ii) 
The Division can approve alternatives to the best 
practices in Section II.H.4.c. where the owner or operator 
demonstrates that the alternatives will achieve equivalent or 
better emission reductions.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
116 
II.H.4.d. 
Creating or updating operating and maintenance plans to 
provide for the use, where practicable, of the following best 
practices. The operating and maintenance plan must describe the 
situations and circumstances where use of the best practice is, and 
is not, practicable, and must identify the documentation that will 
enable the Division to confirm whether the best practice was used 
consistently with the operating and maintenance plan. 
II.H.4.d.(i) 
Using short pig barrels, where it reduces the gas 
volume for potential release. 
II.H.4.d.(ii) 
Planning for venting-reduction steps, such as pipeline 
pump-downs techniques (e.g., in-line compressors, portable 
compressors, ejector), when large vessels and pipelines 
need to be isolated and depressurized. 
II.H.4.d.(iii) 
Minimizing the volume that must be released. For 
example, adding stops to isolate a smaller section of a 
pipeline to reduce the length of pipe that must be vented. 
II.H.4.d.(iv) 
Using inert gases and pigs to perform pipeline purges. 
II.H.4.d.(v) 
Hot tapping to make new connections to pipelines. 
II.H.4.d.(vi) 
Coordinating operational repairs and routine 
maintenance to minimize the number of emissions events 
and volume. 
II.H.5. Recordkeeping. The owner or operator must maintain records for a period 
of five (5) years and make them available to the Division upon request, 
including 
II.H.5.a. 
General records. 
II.H.5.a.(i) 
If subject to Sections II.H.1.a. or II.H.1.b., 
documentation of the methods used to comply with Sections 
II.H.1.a. or II.H.1.b. If exempt from Sections II.H.1.a. or 
II.H.1.b., documentation supporting the exemption. 
II.H.5.a.(ii) 
If control equipment is used to comply with Sections 
II.H.1.a., II.H.1.b., or II.H.1.d., documentation of operating 
and maintenance activities, and the date and duration of any 
control equipment downtime during active pigging operations 
or blowdowns.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
117 
II.H.5.a.(iii) 
Documentation of best practices employed pursuant 
to Section II.H.4., including any operating and maintenance 
plans created, updated, or revised under Section II.H.4.d. 
and the records documenting compliance therewith. 
II.H.5.b. 
Records of pigging operations. 
II.H.5.b.(i) 
The number of pigging events, whether or not subject 
to capture or control, including the locations of the pigging 
event, associated pigging units and facility(ies) (including 
AIRS ID, if applicable); date and time; diameter and normal 
operating pressure of pigging pipeline; pressure of pigging 
unit immediately before and after pigging operations (or after 
capture and recovery if applicable); volume of gas recovered 
and released; and type and volume of liquid removed from 
the pigging unit after pigging operations, if any. 
II.H.5.b.(ii) 
The monthly and annual VOC and methane 
emissions associated with the pigging operations, in 
accordance with Division-approved calculation methodology, 
including the VOC and methane weight percent composition 
of the fluid transported by the pigging pipeline at normal 
pipeline operating conditions used in the calculations and the 
date and location of the sample, or other justification of 
representative composition data. 
II.H.5.c. 
Records of blowdowns. 
II.H.5.c.(i) 
The location (by equipment, facility, and AIRS ID, or 
by equipment and coordinates if no AIRS ID), date and time 
of blowdown event. 
II.H.5.c.(ii) 
The monthly and annual VOC and methane 
emissions from blowdowns, aggregated by equipment 
blown-down. 
II.H.5.c.(iii) 
The date, location, identification of equipment or 
piping and number of blowdown events (other than pigging 
operations), including identification of whether the volume 
between isolation valves is less than 50 cf. 
II.I. 
(State Only) Control of emissions from natural gas-processing plants 
II.I.1. Beginning January 1, 2023, owners or operators of natural gas-processing 
plants that are not subject to the requirements of Section I.G. must comply 
with the leak detection and repair (LDAR) program as provided at 40 CFR 
Part 60, Subpart OOOOa (July 31, 2025) unless subject to the LDAR

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
118 
program provided at 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOO (July 31, 2025). In 
addition, 
II.I.1.a. 
The owner or operator must complete repair of components 
placed on delay of repair within two (2) years or the applicable 
timeline provided in 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOO (July 31, 
2025) or 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOa (July 31, 2025), 
whichever is earlier. 
II.I.1.b. 
The owner or operator must take action(s) to mitigate 
emissions from leaks placed on delay of repair where technically 
feasible. 
II.I.1.c. 
This Section II.I.1. does not apply to process unit equipment 
at natural gas processing plants complying with Section II.I.2. or 40 
CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOb (December 3, 2025). 
II.I.2. Beginning January 1, 2027, owners or operators of process unit 
equipment at natural gas-processing plants must comply with the following 
leak detection and repair (LDAR) program, except that owners or 
operators of process unit equipment subject to 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart 
OOOO (July 31, 2025) or 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOa (July 31, 
2025) must comply with the following LDAR program beginning twelve 
(12) months after EPA approves Colorado’s 111(d) Plan for Crude Oil and 
Natural Gas Facilities. Process unit equipment subject to 40 CFR Part 60, 
Subpart OOOOb (December 3, 2025) are not subject to this Section II.I.2. 
II.I.2.a. 
Definitions. 
II.I.2.a.(i) 
“Commercial unavailability" includes, but is not limited 
to, considerations of valve type and size needed for 
installation, replacement, or repacking; valve operating 
conditions; compatibility of materials of construction; packing 
friction; whether the valve is part of a packaged system; 
retrofit requirements (e.g. re-piping or space limitations); and 
valve vendor, distributor, or manufacturer recommendations. 
II.I.2.a.(ii) 
“Equipment” means each pump, pressure relief 
device, open-ended valve or line, valve, and flange or other 
connector that has the potential to emit methane and any 
device or system required by this Section II.I.2. 
II.I.2.a.(iii) 
“Existing valve” means a valve that is installed at a 
natural gas processing plant before the process unit in which 
the valve is installed becomes subject to this Section II.I.2. 
II.I.2.a.(iv) 
“First attempt at repair” means an action taken for the 
purpose of stopping or reducing fugitive emissions to the

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
119 
atmosphere. First attempts at repair include, but are not 
limited to, the following practices where practicable and 
appropriate: Tightening bonnet bolts; replacing bonnet bolts; 
tightening packing gland nuts; or injecting lubricant into 
lubricated packing. 
II.I.2.a.(v) 
“Low-e valve” means a valve (including its specific 
packing assembly) for which the manufacturer has issued a 
written warranty or performance guarantee that it will not 
emit fugitives at greater than 100 ppm in the first five years 
after being placed in service. A valve may qualify as a low-e 
valve if it is as an extension of another valve that has 
qualified as a low-e valve. 
II.I.2.a.(vi) 
“Low-e packing” means a valve packing product for 
which the manufacturer has issued a written warranty or 
performance guarantee that it will not emit fugitives at 
greater than 100 ppm in the first five years after installation. 
Low-e injectable packing is a type of low-e packing product 
for which the manufacturer has also issued a written 
warranty or performance guarantee and that can be injected 
into a valve during a “drill-and-tap” repair of the valve. 
II.I.2.a.(vii) 
“New valve” means a valve that is installed at a 
natural gas processing plant on or after the date that the 
process unit in which the valve is installed becomes subject 
to this Section II.I.2. This includes valves newly added to the 
process unit, valves installed to replace existing valves, and 
existing valves that are repacked. 
II.I.2.a.(viii) 
“Optical gas imaging” (OGI) means, for this Section 
II.I.2., an instrument that meets the specifications in 
Appendix K to 40 C.F.R. Part 60 (March 8, 2024). 
II.I.2.a.(ix) 
“Process unit” means components assembled for the 
extraction of natural gas liquids from field gas, the 
fractionation of the liquids into natural gas products, or other 
operations associated with the processing of natural gas 
products. A process unit can operate independently if 
supplied with sufficient feed or raw materials and sufficient 
storage facilities for the products. 
II.I.2.a.(x) 
“Process unit shutdown” means a work practice or 
operational procedure that stops production from a process 
unit or part of a process unit during which it is technically 
feasible to clear process material from a process unit or part 
of a process unit consistent with safety constraints and

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
120 
during which repairs can be accomplished. The following are 
not considered process unit shutdowns. 
II.I.2.a.(x)(A) An unscheduled work practice or operational 
procedure that stops production from a process unit 
or part of a process unit for less than 24 hours. 
II.I.2.a.(x)(B) An unscheduled work practice or operational 
procedure that would stop production from a process 
unit or part of a process unit for a shorter period of 
time than would be required to clear the process unit 
or part of the process unit of materials and start up 
the unit, and would result in greater emissions than 
delay of repair of leaking components until the next 
scheduled process unit shutdown. 
II.I.2.a.(x)(C) The use of spare equipment and technically 
feasible bypassing of equipment without stopping 
production. 
II.I.2.b. 
Equipment applicability. 
II.I.2.b.(i) 
Each piece of equipment is presumed to have the 
potential to emit methane unless an owner or operator 
demonstrates that the piece of equipment does not have the 
potential to emit methane. For a piece of equipment to be 
considered not to have the potential to emit methane, the 
methane content of a gaseous stream must be below 
detection limits using Method 18 (April 1, 2025). 
II.I.2.b.(ii) 
As an alternative to Section II.I.2.b.(i), the owner or 
operator may choose to determine that the methane content 
of the stream for equipment in wet gas service is below the 
detection limit of the methods described in ASTM E168-16 
(R20203), E169-16 (R2022), or E260-96 (April 10, 1996). 
II.I.2.b.(iii) 
The owner or operator must demonstrate that a piece 
of equipment is in light liquid service by showing that the 
vapor pressure of one or more of the organic components is 
greater than 0.3 kPa at 20 °C (1.2 in. H2O) at 68 °F), the 
total concentration of the pure organic components having a 
vapor pressure greater than 0.3 kPa at 20 °C is equal to or 
greater than 20 percent by weight, and the fluid is a liquid at 
operating conditions. 
II.I.2.b.(iv) 
As an alternative to Section II.I.2.b.(iii), the owner or 
operator may determine that equipment is in heavy liquid 
service if the weight percent evaporated is 10 percent or less

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
121 
at 150 °C (302 °F)) as determined by ASTM D86-96 (April 
10, 1996) and in light liquid service if the weight percent 
evaporated is greater than 10 percent at 150 °C (302 °F) as 
determined by ASTM D86-96. 
II.I.2.c. 
Inspection frequency. 
II.I.2.c.(i) 
Each pump in light liquid service, pressure relief 
device in gas/vapor service, valve in gas/vapor or light liquid 
service, and connector in gas/vapor or light liquid service 
must be inspected for leaks. 
II.I.2.c.(i)(A) An inspection conducted using OGI in 
accordance with 40 CFR Part 60, Appendix K (May 7, 
2024) must be conducted bimonthly (i.e., every other 
month); or 
II.I.2.c.(i)(B) An inspection conducted using Method 21 
(August 3, 2017) must be conducted 
II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(1) 
Monthly for pumps in light liquid 
service. 
II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(2) 
Quarterly for pressure relief 
devices in gas/vapor service. 
II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(3) 
Quarterly for valves in gas/vapor 
or light liquid service. 
II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(4) 
Annually for connectors in 
gas/vapor service or in light liquid service. 
Inaccessible, ceramic, or ceramic-line 
connectors and connectors that are part of an 
instrumentation system are exempt from the 
annual inspection requirement. A connector is 
inaccessible if it is buried, insulated in a 
manner that prevents access to the connector 
by a monitor probe, obstructed by equipment 
or piping that prevents access to the connector 
by a monitor probe, unable to be reached from 
a wheeled scissor-lift or hydraulic-type scaffold 
that would allow access to connectors up to 7.6 
meters (25 feet) above the ground, would 
require elevating monitoring personnel more 
than 2 meters (7 feet) above a permanent 
support surface or would require the erection of 
scaffold, or is not able to be accessed at any 
time in a same manner (e.g., use of a wheeled

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
122 
scissor-lift on unstable or uneven terrain, the 
use of a motorized man-lift basket in areas 
where an ignition potential exists, or access 
would require near proximity to hazards such 
as electrical lines or would risk damage to 
equipment) to perform monitoring. 
II.I.2.c.(i)(C) Reserved. 
II.I.2.c.(ii) 
Exemptions and exceptions. 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(A) Pressure relief devices routed to a process or 
fuel gas system are exempt from the inspection 
requirement in Section II.I.2.c.(i)(B). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(B) Pressure relief devices equipped with a rupture 
disk, provided the owner or operator installs a new 
rupture disk upstream of the pressure relief device as 
soon as practicable but no later than five (5) calendar 
days after each pressure release, are exempt from 
the inspection requirement in Section II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(1). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(C) Pumps equipped with a dual mechanical seal 
system that includes a barrier fluid system are exempt 
from the inspection requirement in Section 
II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(2) if each dual mechanical seal system is 
operated with the barrier fluid at a pressure that is at 
all times greater than the pump stuffing box pressure, 
is equipped with a barrier fluid degassing reservoir 
that is routed to a process of fuel gas system or is 
equipped with a system that purges the barrier fluid 
into a process stream with zero methane emissions to 
atmosphere; the barrier fluid system is in heavy liquid 
service or does not have the potential to emit 
methane; and each barrier fluid system is equipped 
with a sensor that is checked daily or equipped with 
an audible alarm and that will detect failure of the seal 
system, barrier fluid system, or both. 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(D) Pumps from which emissions are not identified, 
meaning an annual Method 21 (Aug. 3, 2017) reading 
is less than 500 ppmv above background, and that 
have no externally actuated shaft penetrating the 
pump housing are exempt from the inspection 
requirement in Section II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(2). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(E) Reserved.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
123 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(F) Pumps designated as unsafe-to-monitor 
because monitoring personnel would be exposed to 
an immediate danger during monitoring and the 
owner or operator has a written plan to monitor the 
pump as frequently as practicable during safe-to-
monitor times, but not more frequently than the 
periodic monitoring schedule otherwise applicable, 
are exempt from the inspection requirement in 
Section II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(2). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(G) Pumps in light liquid service, pressure relief 
devices in gas/vapor service, valves in gas/vapor and 
light liquid service, and connectors in gas/vapor 
service and in light liquid service that are located at a 
nonfractionating plant that does not have the design 
capacity to process 283,000 scmd (10 million scf per 
day) are exempt from the monitoring requirements in 
Sections II.I.2.c.(i)(A) and II.I.2.c.(i)(B). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(H) Valves in gas/vapor or light liquid service 
designated as unsafe-to-monitor because monitoring 
personnel would be exposed to an immediate danger 
during monitoring and the owner or operator has a 
written plan to monitor the valve as frequently as 
practicable during safe-to-monitor times, but not more 
frequently than the periodic monitoring schedule 
otherwise applicable, are exempt from the inspection 
requirement in Section II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(3). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(I) Valves in gas/vapor or light liquid service 
designated as difficult-to-monitor because the valve 
cannot be monitored without elevating the monitoring 
personnel more than two (2) meters above a support 
service, the process unit within which the valve is 
located has less than 3.0 percent of its total number 
of valves designated as difficult-to-monitor, and the 
owner or operator has a written plan to monitor the 
valve at least once per calendar year are exempt from 
the inspection requirement in Section II.I.2.c.(i)(B)(3). 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(J) Equipment that is designated in vacuum 
service, except connectors in gas/vapor or light liquid 
service, is exempt from the requirements in Section 
II.I.2.c. 
II.I.2.c.(ii)(K) Equipment that is designated as having the 
potential to emit methane less than 300 hr/yr and has

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
124 
the potential to emit methane only during startup and 
shutdown, has the potential to emit methane only 
during process malfunctions or other emergencies, or 
is backup equipment that has the potential to emit 
methane only when the primary equipment is out of 
service is exempt from the requirements in Section 
II.I.2.c. 
II.I.2.c.(iii) 
Owners or operators must also conduct weekly visual 
inspections of each pump in light liquid service for 
indications of liquids dripping from the pump seal. 
II.I.2.c.(iii)(A) Reserved. 
II.I.2.c.(iii)(B) Pumps located within the boundary of an 
unmanned plant site are exempt from the weekly 
visual inspection requirement but must be inspected 
as often as practicable and at least bimonthly (i.e., 
every other month). 
II.I.2.c.(iv) 
Owners or operators must also monitor each pressure 
relief device in gas/vapor service for leaks within five (5) 
calendar days after the owner or operator has detected a 
pressure release. 
II.I.2.c.(iv)(A) Pressure relief devices located in a 
nonfractionating plant that is monitored only by non-
plant personnel may be monitored after a pressure 
release the next time the monitoring personnel are 
onsite or within thirty (30) days after a pressure 
release, whichever is sooner but not longer than thirty 
(30) days. 
II.I.2.c.(iv)(B) Pressure relief devices routed to a process or 
fuel gas system are exempt from the inspection 
requirement in Section II.I.2.c.(iv). 
II.I.2.d. 
Open-ended valves and lines. 
II.I.2.d.(i) 
Owners or operators must equip open-ended valves 
or lines with a cap, blind flange, plug, or second valve that  
seals the open end of the valve or line at all times except 
during operations requiring process fluid flow through the 
open-ended valve or line. 
II.I.2.d.(i)(A) Open-ended valves or lines equipped with a 
second valve must be operated such that the valve on

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
125 
the process fluid end is closed before the second 
valve is closed. 
II.I.2.d.(i)(B) When a double block-and-bleed system is 
used, the bleed valve or line may remain open during 
operations that require venting the line between the 
block valves but must remain closed at all other times. 
II.I.2.d.(i)(C) Open-ended valves or lines in an emergency 
shutdown system that are designed to open 
automatically in the event of a process upset are 
exempt from the requirement in Section II.I.2.d.(i). 
II.I.2.d.(i)(D) Open-ended valves or lines containing 
materials that would autocatalytically polymerize or 
present an explosion, serious overpressure, or other 
safety hazard if capped or equipped with a double 
block-and-bleed system are exempt from the 
requirement in Section II.I.2.d.(i). 
II.I.2.e. 
Reserved. 
II.I.2.f.  
Reserved. 
II.I.2.g. 
Leaks requiring repair. 
II.I.2.g.(i) 
A leak is detected if, during an inspection required by 
this section 
II.I.2.g.(i)(A) For inspections conducted using OGI or AVO, 
any emissions are observed; or 
II.I.2.g.(i)(B) For inspections conducted using the 
procedures of Method 21 (August 3, 2017), 
II.I.2.g.(i)(B)(1) 
A concentration at or above 
2,000 ppmv is detected for a pump in light 
liquid service; 
II.I.2.g.(i)(B)(2) 
A concentration at or above 500 
ppmv is detected for a pressure relief device in 
gas/vapor service; 
II.I.2.g.(i)(B)(3) 
A concentration at or above 500 
ppmv is detected for a valve or connector in 
gas/vapor and light liquid service; and 
II.I.2.g.(i)(B)(4) 
Reserved.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
126 
II.I.2.g.(i)(C) For pumps, valves, and connectors in heavy 
liquid service and pressure relief devices in light liquid 
or heavy liquid service, a leak is detected if evidence 
of a potential leak is found at any time by AVO or 
other detection method. Owners or operators must 
II.I.2.g.(i)(C)(1) 
Monitor the equipment within five 
(5) calendar days using EPA Method 21 
(August 3, 2017). A concentration at or above 
10,000 ppmv is considered a leak requiring 
repair; or 
II.I.2.g.(i)(C)(2) 
Designate the evidence of a 
potential leak as a leak requiring repair in 
accordance with Section II.I.2.h. 
II.I.2.g.(i)(D) If there are indications of liquids dripping from 
the pump seal of a pump in light liquid service 
observed during the weekly visual inspection, the 
owner or operator must 
II.I.2.g.(i)(D)(1) 
Monitor the pump within five (5) 
calendar days of observing the liquids dripping 
using OGI or Method 21 (August 3, 2017). A 
leak is detected if any emissions are observed 
using OGI or if a concentration at or above 
2,000 ppmv is observed with Method 21; or, 
II.I.2.g.(i)(D)(2) 
Designate the visual indications 
of liquids dripping as a leak and repair in 
accordance with Section II.I.2.h. 
II.I.2.g.(i)(E) A leak is detected at a pressure relief device 
monitored following a pressure release if any 
emissions are observed using OGI or if a 
concentration at or above 500 ppmv is observed with 
Method 21 (August 3, 2017). 
II.I.2.g.(i)(F) A leak detected at an open-ended valve or line 
when the valve or line is sealed or closed must be 
repaired. Any emissions observed using OGI or 
emissions detected at a concentration at or above 
500 ppmv observed with Method 21 (August 3, 2017) 
is a leak. 
II.I.2.g.(i)(G) A leak detected at a pump, valve, or connector 
in heavy liquid service or at a pressure relief device in 
light liquid or heavy liquid service must be repaired.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
127 
Any emissions observed using OGI or AVO or 
emissions detected at a concentration at or above 
10,000 ppmv observed with Method 21 (August 3, 
2017) is a leak. 
II.I.2.h. 
Repair, replacement, and remonitoring. 
II.I.2.h.(i) 
The owner or operator must repair leaks in 
accordance with this Section II.I.2.h., including valves 
subject to Section II.I.2.h.(ii). 
II.I.2.h.(i)(A) The owner or operator must attach a 
weatherproof and readily visible identification tag 
marked with the equipment identification number to 
the leaking equipment until the leak is repaired. 
II.I.2.h.(i)(B) The owner or operator must make a first 
attempt to repair the leak no later than five (5) 
calendar days after a leak is detected. A first attempt 
at repair is not required if the leak is detected using 
OGI and the equipment identified as leaking would 
require elevating the repair personnel more than two 
(2) meters above a support surface. 
II.I.2.h.(i)(C) Leaks must be repaired within fifteen (15) 
calendar days after detection, except as provided in 
Sections II.I.2.h.(i)(D), II.I.2.h.(i)(E), or II.I.2.h.(iii). 
II.I.2.h.(i)(D) Repairs of visual indication of liquids dripping 
from pumps in light liquid service that can be made by 
eliminating visual indications of liquids dripping must 
be made within five (5) calendar days after dripping is 
observed. 
II.I.2.h.(i)(E) Repairs of leaks detected at an open-ended 
valve or line; at a pump, valve, or connector in heavy 
liquid service; or at a pressure relief device in light 
liquid or heavy liquid service that can be made by 
eliminating the indication of the potential leak must be 
made within five (5) calendar days after detection. 
II.I.2.h.(ii) 
Required installation, replacement, or repacking of 
low-e valves or low-e packing 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(A) A new valve must be either a low-e valve or 
fitted with low-e packing.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
128 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(B) An existing valve that is a non-low-e valve or 
valve not fitted with low-e packing must be either 
replaced by a low-e valve or fitted with low-e packing 
if 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(B)(1) 
Emissions from the valve are 
detected at a concentration at or above 10,000 
ppm during an inspection conducted using 
Method 21 (August 3, 2017). 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(B)(2) 
Any emissions from the valve are 
observed during an inspection conducted using 
OGI, unless the owner or operator 
demonstrates through Method 21 monitoring 
that the emissions are below 10,000 ppm. 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(B)(3) 
Valves that have been replaced 
with a low-e valve or repacked with low-e 
packing pursuant to Sections II.I.2.h.(ii)(B)(1) 
or II.B.2.h.(ii)(B)(2) that are found to be leaking 
per Section II.I.2.g.(i) are subject to the repair 
requirements in Section II.I.2.h.(i). 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(C) Valves that require replacement with a low-e 
valve or repacking with low-e packing pursuant to 
Sections II.I.2.h.(ii)(A) or II.I.2.h.(ii)(B) must be 
replaced or repacked before the end of the next 
process unit shutdown, except 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(C)(1) 
As provided in Section II.I.2.h.(iii); 
or 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(C)(2) 
Where two consecutive 
inspections conducted pursuant to Section 
II.I.2.c. demonstrate that emissions are below 
the levels requiring repair specified in Section 
II.I.2.g. 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(D) An owner or operator is not required to utilize a 
low-e valve or low-e packing to replace or repack a 
valve where the low-e valve or low-e packing is not 
technically feasible. A low-e valve or low-e packing 
that is not suitable for its intended use is considered 
to be technically infeasible. Additional factors that 
may be considered in determining technical 
infeasibility include retrofit requirements for 
installation (e.g., re-piping or space limitation),

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
129 
commercial unavailability for valve type, or certain 
instrumentation assemblies. 
II.I.2.h.(ii)(E) Valves that have been replaced with a low-e 
valve or repacked with low-e packing pursuant to 
Sections II.I.2.h.(ii)(A) or II.I.2.h.(ii)(B) that are found 
to be leaking pursuant to an inspection conducted in 
accordance with Section II.I.2.g. must be repaired as 
specified in Section II.I.2.h.(i). 
II.I.2.h.(iii) 
Delay of repair. 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(A) The owner or operator may delay repair of a 
leak, if 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(A)(1) 
It is technically infeasible to 
conduct the repair without a process unit 
shutdown; 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(A)(2) 
The equipment requiring repair is 
isolated from the process and does not have 
the potential to emit methane; 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(A)(3) 
The owner or operator 
demonstrates that the emissions of purged 
material resulting from immediate repair of a 
valve or connector is greater than the fugitive 
emissions likely to result from the delay of 
repair, so long as when repair is conducted the 
purged material is collected and destroyed or 
recovered in a control device; or 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(A)(4) 
The repair procedures for a pump 
require the use of a dual mechanical seal 
system that includes a barrier fluid system and 
repair is completed as soon as practicable but 
no later than six (6) months after detection. 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(B) Leaks on delay of repair because repair 
requires a process unit shutdown must be repaired 
before the end of the next process unit shutdown 
unless the next process unit shutdown for a natural 
gas processing plant located in an ozone 
nonattainment area is between June and August, in 
which case the process unit shutdown and associated 
repair may be delayed until September of the same 
calendar year.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
130 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(C) Leaks on delay of repair because the valve 
must be repacked or replaced may be delayed. 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(D) Delay of repair beyond the next process unit 
shutdown is allowed for a valve if valve assembly 
replacement is necessary during the process unit 
shutdown, valve assembly supplies at the natural gas 
processing plant have been depleted, and valve 
assembly supplies had been sufficiently stocked 
before the supplies were depleted. This delay is not 
allowed unless the next process unit shutdown occurs 
sooner than six (6) months after the first process unit 
shutdown. 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(E) Leaks at pumps, valves, or connectors on 
delay of repair may be considered to be repaired and 
no longer on delay of repair if two consecutive 
bimonthly monitoring shows that no leak remains. 
II.I.2.h.(iii)(F) The owner or operator must take action(s) to 
mitigate emissions from leaks placed on delay of 
repair where technically feasible. 
II.I.2.h.(iv) 
Within fifteen (15) calendar days of completion of a 
repair, the leak must be remonitored using OGI or EPA 
Method 21 (August 3, 2017) to verify that the repair was 
effective. Pumps in light liquid service repaired in 
accordance with Section II.I.2.h.(i)(D) are not subject to 
remonitoring to verify repair. 
II.I.2.i Recordkeeping. 
II.I.2.i.(i) 
Owners or operators must maintain the following 
records for at least five (5) years and make records available 
to the Division upon request. 
II.I.2.i.(i)(A) Records of process unit identification for each 
process unit and date the process unit becomes 
subject to this Section II.I.2. 
II.I.2.i.(i)(B) Records of process unit equipment monitoring 
inspections including the date and times of the 
inspection, inspector name, leak determination 
method, type of equipment monitored, process unit 
identification, and applicable OGI inspection records 
in 40 CFR Part 60, Appendix K (May 7, 2024).

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
131 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C) Records of leaks detected and repaired, 
replaced, or repacked, including 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(1) 
The instrument and operator 
identification numbers; 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(2) 
The process unit and equipment 
identification numbers; 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(3) 
The date the leak was detected; 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(4) 
The date and repair method for 
the first attempt to repair and for the final repair 
of the leak; 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(5) 
The date of remonitoring to verify 
repair and the monitoring method used to 
confirm the repair; 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(6) 
The reason for delay of repair or 
delay of valve replacement or repacking and 
expected date of successful repair; and 
II.I.2.i.(i)(C)(7) 
Equipment identification numbers 
of equipment from which emissions are not 
identified pursuant to Section II.I.2.c.(ii)(D), 
designated as unsafe-to-monitor or difficult-to-
monitor, designated in vacuum service, as 
having the potential to emit methane less than 
300 hr/yr, and valves determined infeasible to 
replace or repack with low-e valves or packing 
technology and the reason why it was 
infeasible. 
II.I.2.i.(i)(D) Records of visual inspections of pumps in light 
liquid service including the date of the inspection, 
inspector name, and the result of the inspection (i.e., 
indications of liquids dripping or no indication of 
liquids dripping). 
II.I.2.i.(i)(E) Dates of installation, repair, or repacking, as 
applicable, of low-e valves and low-e packing. 
II.I.2.i.(i)(F) 
Records of manufacturer low-e valve and low-e 
packing written warranty or performance guarantee 
for each type or class of low-e valve and low-e 
packing used to comply with Section II.I.2.h.(ii).

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
132 
II.I.2.j. Reporting 
II.I.2.j.(i) 
In addition to the information required to be reported 
pursuant to Regulation Number 7, Part B, Section V., owners 
or operators must report, semi-annually 
II.I.2.j.(i)(A) Company name, physical street address, and 
name and contact information of the company 
representative, for reporting purposes. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(B) The date of submittal and the period covered 
by the report. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(C) For each process unit, process unit 
identification and number of valves, pumps, 
connectors, and pressure relief devices subject to 
monitoring. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(D) For each process unit, the number of valves; 
pumps; connectors; pressure relief devices; open-
ended valves or lines; and pumps, valves, or 
connectors in heavy liquid service or pressure relief 
devices in light liquid or heavy liquid service for which 
leaks were detected. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(E) For each process unit, the number of valves; 
pumps; connectors; pressure relief devices; open-
ended valves or lines; and pumps, valves, or 
connectors in heavy liquid service or pressure relief 
devices in light liquid or heavy liquid service for which 
leaks were not repaired. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(F) 
Explanation for each delay of repair or delay of 
valve replacement or repacking and, where 
appropriate, why the repair, replacement, or 
repacking was technically infeasible without a process 
unit shutdown. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(G) Dates of process unit shutdowns. 
II.I.2.j.(i)(H) Certification by a certifying official of truth, 
accuracy, and completeness of the report. 
II.J. 
Wet and Dry Seal Centrifugal Compressors 
II.J.1. Beginning January 1, 2027, owners or operators of centrifugal 
compressors, including those equipped with wet or dry seals, in the crude 
oil and natural gas source category must begin to implement one of the

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
133 
methane emissions requirement options specified in Sections II.J.1.a 
through II.J.1.d., unless subject to the centrifugal compressor 
requirements in 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOb (December 3, 2025). 
Beginning six (6) months after the date EPA approves Colorado’s 111(d) 
Plan for Crude Oil and Natural Gas Facilities, these provisions apply to 
centrifugal compressors subject to 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOO (July 
31, 2025) or 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOa (July 31, 2025). These 
provisions do not apply to centrifugal compressors located at a well 
production facility, unless the well production facility is classified as a 
centralized well production facility, pursuant to Section II.A.5. of this Part 
B. 
II.J.1.a. 
Control of wet seal emissions. 
II.J.1.a.(i) 
If volumetric flow rate, measured according to Section 
II.J.1.c., from each wet seal, including self contained wet 
seals, exceeds 3 standard cubic feet per minute (scfm) per 
seal, the owner or operator must complete repair in 
accordance with Section II.J.1.e. If multiple seals are 
manifolded to a single open-vented line, the total volumetric 
flow rate threshold that triggers Section II.J.1.e. repair 
requirements is 3 scfm multiplied by the number of seals 
connected to the vent. 
II.J.1.a.(i)(A) Initial volumetric flow rate measurements must 
be conducted on or before 8,760 hours of operation 
after the applicable compliance date in Section II.J.1., 
or on or before 8,760 hours of operation after 
compressor startup, whichever date is later. 
Measurements must be made in accordance with 
II.J.1.c. 
II.J.1.a.(i)(B) Subsequent measurements must be conducted 
every 8,760 hours of operation after the date of the 
previous measurement. 
II.J.1.b. 
Control of dry seal emissions. 
II.J.1.b.(i) 
If volumetric flow rate limit, measured according to 
Section II.J.1.c., from each dry seal, exceeds 10 scfm per 
seal, the owner or operator must complete repair in 
accordance with Section II.J.1.e. If multiple dry seals are 
manifolded to a single open-vented line, the total volumetric 
flow rate threshold that triggers Section II.J.1.e. repair 
requirements is 10 scfm multiplied by the number of seals 
connected to the vent.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
134 
II.J.1.b.(i)(A) Initial volumetric flow rate measurements must 
be conducted on or before 8,760 hours of operation 
determined after the applicable compliance date in 
Section II.J.1., or on or before 8,760 hours of 
operation after compressor startup, whichever date is 
later. Measurements must be made in accordance 
with II.J.1.c. 
II.J.1.b.(i)(B) Subsequent measurements must be conducted 
on or before 8,760 hours of operation after the date of 
the previous measurement. 
II.J.1.c. 
Volumetric flow rate measurement. 
II.J.1.c.(i) 
If complying with the volumetric flow rate 
requirements in Section II.J.1.a. or Section II.J.1.b., for each 
dry or wet seal centrifugal compressor in operating-mode or 
in standby-pressurized-mode, volumetric flow rate at 
standard conditions from each dry or wet seal vent may be 
determined using any of the methods in Sections 
II.J.1.c.(i)(A) through II.J.1.c.(i)(D). 
II.J.1.c.(i)(A) A temporary or permanent flow meter that 
meets the requirement in EPA Method 2D (August 3, 
2017) and is calibrated on an annual basis according 
to the requirements in Method 2D; 
II.J.1.c.(i)(B) A high-volume sampler according to the 
methods in 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOb, 
Section 60.5386b(c) (December 3, 2025); or 
II.J.1.c.(i)(C) Inspect the dry or wet seal vent for emissions 
during an inspection conducted pursuant to Sections 
II.E. or II.I.2. If any emissions are detected using an 
infra-red camera or OGI, emissions are detected by 
other Division approved instrument based monitoring 
method or program exceeding the leak detection 
identification requiring repair threshold established in 
the approval, or emissions detected greater than 500 
ppmv using EPA Method 21 (August 3, 2017), the 
owner or operator must conduct a volumetric flow rate 
measurement in accordance with Sections 
II.J.1.c.(i)(A), II.J.1.c.(i)(B), II.J.1.c.(i)(D), and 
II.J.1.c.(ii). If no emissions are detected during the 
Section II.E. or II.I.2. inspection, the owner or operator 
may assume the volumetric flow rate is zero.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
135 
II.J.1.c.(i)(D) Any other method that has been validated in 
accordance with the procedures specified in EPA 
Method 301 (October 7, 2020), subject to EPA 
approval. 
II.J.1.c.(ii) 
Measurements conducted on manifolded groups of 
dry or wet seal centrifugal compressors must be taken at a 
single point in the manifold downstream of all dry or wet seal 
compressor inputs and, if practical, prior to comingling with 
other non-compressor emission sources. Measurements 
must be taken in accordance with the methods in Section 
II.J.1.c.(i). 
II.J.1.d. 
As an alternative to the volumetric flow rate requirements for 
wet and dry seals under Sections II.J.1.a. and II.J.1.b., an owner or 
operator may instead comply with either Section II.J.1.d.(i) or 
II.J.1.d.(ii). 
II.J.1.d.(i) 
Route emissions from each centrifugal compressor 
wet seal fluid degassing system to a control device that 
achieves at least 95% methane reduction; or 
II.J.1.d.(ii) 
Route emissions from each wet seal fluid degassing 
system or dry seal system to a process. 
II.J.1.e. 
 
Repair requirements. 
II.J.1.e.(i) 
Except as specified in Section II.J.1.e.(iii), compressor 
seals must be repaired within ninety (90) calendar days after 
the date of the volumetric emissions measurement that 
exceeds the applicable required flow rate per seal. 
II.J.1.e.(ii) 
A follow-up volumetric flow rate measurement must 
be conducted within fifteen (15) operating days after the 
repair to document that the rate has been reduced to less 
than the applicable required flow rate per seal(s). 
II.J.1.e.(iii) 
If repair is technically infeasible, would require a vent 
blowdown, a compressor station shutdown, or would be 
unsafe to repair during operation of the unit, the owner or 
operator may delay repair, and repairs must be completed 
during the next scheduled compressor station shutdown for 
maintenance, after a scheduled vent blowdown, or within 2 
years of the measurement exceeding the applicable flow rate 
per seal, whichever is earliest.  A vent blowdown is the 
opening of one or more blowdown valves to depressurize 
major production and processing equipment, other than a 
storage vessel.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
136 
II.J.1.e.(iv) 
If repair requires replacement of the compressor seal 
or a part but the required parts are unavailable despite prior 
stocking, parts must be ordered within 10 days of adding the 
seal to the delay of the repair list. Repairs must be 
completed as soon as practicable, but no later than 30 days 
after receipt of parts, unless a compressor station shutdown 
is required, in which case comply with Section II.J.1.e.(iii). 
II.J.1.e.(v) 
Reserved. 
II.J.1.f. 
Recordkeeping. 
II.J.1.f.(i) 
Owners or operators must maintain the following 
records  related to the requirements in Part B, Section II.J. 
for at least five (5) years and make records available to the 
Division upon request: 
II.J.1.f.(i)(A) Reserved. 
II.J.1.f.(i)(B) Reserved. 
II.J.1.f.(i)(C) Reserved. 
II.J.1.f.(i)(D) Reserved. 
II.J.1.f.(i)(E) Reserved. 
II.J.1.f.(i)(F) Records of volumetric flow rate measurements 
for wet and dry seal systems, including dates of initial 
and subsequent measurements, measured rates, 
repairs made following exceedances, identification of 
each seal placed on delay of repair and explanation 
for the delay, repair follow-up volumetric flow rate 
measurement results, and a description of the method 
used and the results of the volumetric flow rate 
measurement or emissions screening, as applicable. 
II.J.1.g. 
Annual Reporting. 
II.J.1.g.(i) 
Beginning June 30, 2028, and by June 30 each year 
thereafter, owners or operators must submit annual reports 
for the previous calendar year, in a Division-approved 
format. For centrifugal compressors subject to 40 CFR Part 
60, Subpart OOOO (July 31, 2025) or 40 CFR Part 60, 
Subpart OOOOa (July 31, 2025), the first annual report must 
be submitted by June 30 of the year following the date EPA 
approves Colorado’s 111(d) Plan for Crude Oil and Natural 
Gas Facilities, or June 30, 2028, whichever is later.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
137 
II.J.1.g.(ii) 
Owners or operators operating centrifugal 
compressors must include the following in their annual 
reports: 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(A) Identification of each centrifugal compressor. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(B) For each deviation that occurred during the 
reporting period, the date and time the deviation 
began, its duration in hours, and a description of the 
deviation. If no deviations occurred, provide a 
statement confirming no deviations occurred during 
the reporting period. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(B)(1) 
“Deviation”, for purposes of 
Section II.J., means any instance in which an 
owner or operator of a source subject to 
Sections II.J.1.a. through II.J.1.e. fails to: meet 
any requirement or obligation established by 
Sections II.J.1. including, but not limited to, any 
operating limit or work practice standard; meet 
any term or condition that is adopted to 
implement an applicable requirement in 
Section II.J.1; and/or meet any operating limit, 
or work practice standard of Section II.J.1. 
during startup, shutdown, or malfunction. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(C) The cumulative number of hours of operation 
since initial startup, since the applicable compliance 
date in Section II.J.1., or since the last volumetric flow 
rate measurement conducted pursuant to Sections 
II.J.1.a. or II.J.1.b., or since an inspection for leaks 
pursuant to Section II.J.1.c.(i)(C), as applicable, which 
have elapsed prior to conducting your volumetric flow 
rate measurement pursuant to Section II.J.1.a. or 
II.J.1.b. or inspection for leaks pursuant to Section 
II.J.1.c.(i)(C). 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(D) A description of the method used and the 
results of any volumetric emissions measurement or 
inspection for leaks pursuant to Section II.J.1.c.(i)(C), 
as applicable. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(E) Reserved. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(F) Reserved. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(G) Reserved.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
138 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(H) Reserved. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(I) The number and type of seals placed on delay 
of repair, along with an explanation for each delay. 
II.J.1.g.(ii)(J) The date(s) of any planned shutdown(s) during 
the reporting period if they relate to seals placed on 
delay of repair as of the date of the planned 
shutdown(s). 
II.J.1.h. 
As an alternative to the emission control, inspection, repair, 
and recordkeeping provisions described in Section II.J.1.i., the 
owner or operator may comply with the requirements for centrifugal 
compressor emission control, monitoring, recordkeeping, and 
reporting requirements of 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOb 
(December 3, 2025). 
II.K. 
Reciprocating Compressors 
II.K.1. Beginning January 1, 2027, owners or operators of reciprocating 
compressors in the crude oil and natural gas source category must begin 
to implement one of the methane emissions requirement options specified 
in Sections II.K.1.a. through II.K.1.d., unless subject to the reciprocating 
compressor requirements in 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOb (December 
3, 2025). Beginning six (6) months after EPA approves Colorado’s 111(d) 
Plan for Crude Oil and Natural Gas Facilities, these provisions apply to 
reciprocating compressors subject to 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOO 
(July 31, 2025) or 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOa (July 31, 2025). 
These provisions do not apply to reciprocating compressors located at a 
well production facility, unless the well production facility is classified as a 
centralized well production facility, pursuant to Section II.A.5. of this Part 
B. 
II.K.1.a. 
Control of emissions. 
II.K.1.a.(i) 
If volumetric flow rate, measured according to Section 
II.K.1.b., from each reciprocating compressor cylinder rod 
packing exceeds 2 scfm per cylinder, the owner or operator 
must complete repair or replacement in accordance with 
Section II.K.1.c. If the individual cylinders are manifolded to 
a single open-ended vent line, the volumetric flow rate 
threshold that triggers Section II.K.1.c. repair or replacement 
requirements is 2 scfm multiplied by the number of 
compression cylinders connected to the vent. 
II.K.1.a.(i)(A) Initial volumetric flow rate measurements must 
be conducted on or before 8,760 hours of operation 
after last rod packing replacement, on or before 8,760

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
139 
hours of operation after the applicable compliance 
date in Section II.K.1., or on or before 8,760 hours of 
operation after compressor startup, whichever date is 
later. 
II.K.1.a.(i)(B) Subsequent measurements must be conducted 
on or before 8,760 hours of operation after the date of 
the previous measurement which demonstrates the 
applicable volumetric flow rate per cylinder meets the 
applicable volumetric flow rate in Section II.K.1.a.(i) 
or, on or before 8,760 hours of operation after the last 
rod packing replacement, whichever date is later. 
II.K.1.a.(ii) 
As an alternative to the volumetric flow rate 
requirements in Section II.K.1.a.(i), owners or operators may 
instead comply with one of the options in Sections 
II.K.1.a.(ii)(A), II.K.1.a.(ii)(B), or II.K.1.a.(ii)(C). 
II.K.1.a.(ii)(A) Route emissions from the reciprocating 
compressor rod packing using a rod packing 
emissions collection system to a process; 
II.K.1.a.(ii)(B) Route emissions from each rod packing 
emissions collection system to a control device that 
achieves at least 95% methane reduction; or 
II.K.1.a.(ii)(C) Replace rod packing on or before 8,760 hours 
of operation after the applicable compliance date in 
Section II.K.1., on or before 8,760 hours of operation 
after the previous flow rate measurement, or on or 
before 8,760 hours of operation after the date of the 
most recent rod packing replacement, whichever is 
later. 
II.K.1.a.(ii)(D) Reserved. 
II.K.1.b. 
Volumetric flow rate measurement. 
II.K.1.b.(i) 
If complying with the volumetric flow rate 
requirements in Section II.K.1.a.(i), owners or operators of 
reciprocating compressors equipped with an open-ended 
vent line in operating or standby pressurized mode must 
determine the volumetric flow rate from the open-ended line 
using any of the methods in Sections II.K.1.b.(i)(A) through 
II.K.1.b.(i)(D). 
II.K.1.b.(i)(A) A temporary or permanent flow meter that 
meets the requirement in EPA Method 2D (August 3,

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
140 
2017) and is calibrated on an annual basis according 
to the requirements in Method 2D. 
II.K.1.b.(i)(B) A high-volume sampler designed to capture the 
entirety of the emissions from the applicable vent and 
measure the methane concentrations being emitted 
as well as the total volumetric flow at standard 
conditions according to methods in 40 CFR Part 60, 
Subpart OOOOb, 60.5386b(c) (December 3, 2025). 
II.K.1.b.(i)(C) Inspect the compressor rod packing for 
emissions during an inspection conducted pursuant to 
Sections II.E. or II.I.2. If any emissions are detected 
using an infra-red camera or OGI, emissions are 
detected by other Division approved instrument based 
monitoring method or program exceeding the leak 
detection identification requiring repair threshold 
established in the approval, or emissions detected 
greater than 500 ppmv using EPA Method 21 (August 
3, 2017), the owner or operator must conduct a 
volumetric flow rate measurement in accordance with 
Sections II.K.1.b.(i)(A), II.K.1.b.(i)(B), II.K.1.b.(i)(D), 
and II.K.1.b.(iii). If no emissions are detected during 
the Section II.E. or II.I.2. inspection, the owner or 
operator may assume the volumetric flow rate is zero. 
II.K.1.b.(i)(D) As an alternative to a high-volume sampler, 
you may use any other method that has been 
validated in accordance with the procedures specified 
in EPA Method 301 (October 7, 2020), subject to EPA 
approval. 
II.K.1.b.(ii) 
If complying with Section II.K.1.a.(i), owners or 
operators of reciprocating compressors not equipped with an 
open-ended vent line in operating or standby pressurized 
mode must 
II.K.1.b.(ii)(A) Use the methods described in Section II.E. or 
II.I.2. to conduct leak detection of emissions from the 
rod packing case into an open distance piece, or, for 
compressors with a closed distance piece, conduct 
annual leak detection of emissions from the rod 
packing vent, distance piece vent, compressor crank 
case breather cap, or other vent emitting gas from the 
rod packing.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
141 
II.K.1.b.(ii)(B) Measure emissions found in Section 
II.K.1.b.(ii)(A) using a meter or high-volume sampler 
according to methods in 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart 
OOOOb, 60.5386b(b) or (c) (December 3, 2025). 
II.K.1.b.(ii)(C) As an alternative to a high-volume sampler, 
you may use any other method that has been 
validated in accordance with the procedures specified 
in EPA Method 301 (October 7, 2020), subject to EPA 
approval. 
II.K.1.b.(iii) 
Measurements conducted on manifolded groups of 
reciprocating compressors must be taken at a single point in 
the manifold downstream of all compressor vent inputs and, 
if practical, prior to comingling with other non-compressor 
emission sources. Measurements must be taken in 
accordance with the methods in Sections II.K.1.b.(i) or 
II.K.1.b.(ii). 
II.K.1.c. 
Repair or replacement requirements. 
II.K.1.c.(i) 
Except as specified in Section II.K.1.c.(iii), the rod 
packing must be repaired or replaced within ninety (90) days 
after the date of the volumetric emissions measurement that 
exceeded 2 scfm per cylinder. 
II.K.1.c.(ii) 
A follow-up volumetric flow rate measurement must 
be conducted within fifteen (15) operating days after the 
repair or replacement to document that the rate has been 
reduced to less than 2 scfm per cylinder. 
II.K.1.c.(iii) 
If repair or replacement is technically infeasible, would 
require a vent blowdown, a compressor station shutdown, or 
would be unsafe to repair during operation of the unit, the 
owner or operator may delay repair or replacement. Repair 
must be completed during the next scheduled compressor 
station shutdown for maintenance, after a scheduled vent 
blowdown, or within two (2) years after the volumetric 
emissions measurement that exceeded 2 scfm per cylinder, 
whichever is earliest. A vent blowdown is the opening of one 
or more blowdown valves to depressurize major production 
and processing equipment, other than a storage vessel. 
II.K.1.c.(iv) 
If repair requires replacement of the rod packing or a 
part but the required parts are unavailable despite prior 
stocking, parts must be ordered within 10 days of adding the 
compressor to the delay of repair list. Repairs must be

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
142 
completed as soon as practicable, but no later than 30 days 
after receipt of parts, unless a compressor station shutdown 
is required, in which case comply with Section II.K.1.c.(iii). 
II.K.1.c.(v) 
Reserved. 
II.K.1.c.(vi) 
Reserved. 
II.K.1.c.(vii) Reserved. 
II.K.1.d. 
Recordkeeping. 
II.K.1.d.(i) 
Owners or operators must maintain the following 
records related to the requirements in Section II.K for at least 
five (5) years and make records available to the Division 
upon request. 
II.K.1.d.(i)(A) The cumulative hours of operation since the 
previous rod packing replacement conducted 
pursuant to Section II.K.1.a.(ii), since initial startup, 
since the compliance deadline in Section II.K.1., or 
since the date of the previous volumetric flow rate 
measurement, as applicable; or, a statement that 
emissions from the rod packing are being routed to a 
process; 
II.K.1.d.(i)(B) The date of each rod packing repair or 
replacement, or date of installation of a rod packing 
emissions collection system; 
II.K.1.d.(i)(C) Records of volumetric flow rate measurements, 
including dates of initial and subsequent 
measurements, measured rates, repairs made 
following exceedances, identification of each 
compressor placed on delay of repair and explanation 
for the delay, repair follow-up volumetric flow rate 
measurement results, and a description of the method
