Section 25-7-110.8, C.R.S., requirements do not apply to this rulemaking because the 
adopted rule is not directly intended to reduce air pollution. To the extent § 25-7-110.8, 
C.R.S. does apply to this rulemaking, after considering all the information in the record, 
the Commission hereby makes the determination that: 
(I) 
The rule is based on reasonably available, validated, reviewed, and sound 
scientific methodologies, and the Commission has considered all information 
submitted by interested parties. 
(II) 
The rule is not intended to address a reduction in air pollution, or, in the 
alternative, is administrative in nature. 
(III) 
Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rule provides benefits that 
justify the costs to government to implement the rule, and to the extent possible, 
minimizes any cost to the regulated community or public in order for the 
Commission to establish sufficiently comprehensive and robust inventories of 
TAC. 
(IV) 
The rules are the most cost-effective to achieve the necessary and desired 
results and reduction in air pollution. 
(V) 
The rule does not directly result in air quality benefits but shall address state 
statutory requirements in a cost effective manner.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
551 
GG. 
November 19-21, 2025 (Revisions to Part A, Section I. and Part B, Sections 
I., III., VI., and VII.) 
This Statement of Basis, Specific Statutory Authority, and Purpose complies with the 
requirements of the State Administrative Procedure Act, § 24-4-103(4), C.R.S., the 
Colorado Air Pollution Prevention and Control Act, (State Air Act or the Act), §§ 25-7-
110 and 25-7-110.5., C.R.S., and the Air Quality Control Commission’s (Commission) 
Procedural Rules, 5 Code Colo. Reg. §1001-1. 
Basis 
On July 24, 2024, EPA reclassified the Denver Metro/North Front Range (DM/NFR) and 
northern Weld County ozone nonattainment area to serious for the 2015 8-hour Ozone 
National Ambient Air Quality Standard of 70 parts per billion (ppb) (2015 ozone 
NAAQS). See 89 Fed. Reg. 59832 (July 24, 2024). 
Further, in a March 2023 letter Governor Jared Polis directed the Colorado Department 
of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) and the Energy and Carbon Management 
Commission (ECMC) (fka Colorado Oil and Gas Conservation Commission) to adopt 
rules to reduce nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions from upstream oil and gas operations 
by 30% in 2025 and 50% by 2030, relative to a 2017 baseline. The Commission 
adopted requirements to achieve the 2025 reduction target in 2023. 
To ensure progress towards attainment of the ozone NAAQS and comply with the 
Governor’s directed 2030 reduction goal, the Commission adopted revisions to 
Regulation Number 7 expand state implementation plan (SIP) applicability to additional 
sources in northern Weld County. The Commission also adopted revisions to the use 
and operation of fossil fuel-fired equipment at oil and gas well drilling and completion 
operations. 
Statutory Authority 
The State Air Act, specifically § 25-7-105(1), C.R.S., directs the Commission to 
promulgate such rules and regulations as are consistent with the legislative declaration 
set forth in § 25-7-102, C.R.S., and that are necessary for the proper implementation 
and administration of Article 7. The Act broadly defines air pollutant to include 
essentially any gas emitted into the atmosphere (and, as such, includes volatile organic 
compounds (VOC), NOx, methane and other hydrocarbons) and provides the 
Commission broad authority to regulate air pollutants. Section 105(1)(a)(I) directs the 
Commission to adopt a SIP to attain the NAAQS. Section 105(1)(b) directs the 
Commission to adopt emission control regulations in conformity with § 25-7-109. C.R.S. 
Section 25-7-106, C.R.S., provides the Commission maximum flexibility in developing 
an effective air quality program and promulgating such combination of regulations as 
may be necessary or desirable to carry out that program. Section 25-7-106 also 
authorizes the Commission to promulgate emission control regulations applicable to the 
entire state, specified areas or zones, or a specified class of pollution. Section 25-7-
106(6), C.R.S., further authorizes the Commission to require owners and operators of

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
552 
any air pollution source to monitor, record, and report information. Section 25-7-
109(1)(a), C.R.S., directs the Commission to adopt emission control requirements that 
require the use of effective practical air pollution controls for: (1) each significant source 
or category of significant sources of air pollutants and (2) each type of facility, process, 
or activity which products or might product significant emissions of air pollutants. 
Section 25-7-103(11), C.R.S., defines emission control regulation to mean a regulation 
that is applicable to a specified type of facility, process, or activity for the purpose of 
controlling the extent, degree, or nature of pollution emitted and a regulation that adopts 
any design, equipment, work practice, or operational standard. Section 25-7-109(2), 
C.R.S., allows emission control regulations pertaining to, among other pollutants, 
nitrogen oxides and hydrocarbons. Section 25-7-109(10)(d), C.R.S., directs the Division 
to propose rules on or before August 31, 2026, designed to reduce emissions of NOx 
generated by upstream oil and gas operations by fifty percent by 2030 relative to the 
2017 NOx emission level in the SIP. 
Purpose 
The following section sets forth the Commission’s purpose in adopting the revisions to 
Regulation Number 7 and includes the technological and scientific rationale for the 
adoption of the revisions. 
Oil and gas pre-production operations 
In 2023, the Commission adopted rules to reduce the impact of the use and operation of 
fossil fuel-fired equipment at oil and gas drilling, hydraulic fracturing, and hydraulic 
refracturing operations. The regulations were adopted to secure a 30% reduction in 
upstream oil and gas NOx emissions by 2025 and move the area towards attainment 
with federal ozone standards. The Commission adopted these revisions recognizing 
they are one step in meeting the Governor’s directive of achieving a 50% reduction in 
NOx emissions from the upstream oil and gas sector by 2030. The Commission also 
directed the establishment of a NOx reduction steering committee to evaluate the 2026 
NOx intensity target and use practices and opportunities to meet the later 2030 
emissions reduction goal. 
The Commission now adopts additional NOx intensity targets to achieve a 50% 
reduction by and beyond 2030. The Commission revised the reporting requirements to 
retain the requirement for operators to report NOx emissions and production for the 
Division to use to evaluate progress toward the NOx intensity targets. The Commission 
directs the Division to annually evaluate actual production data. The Division’s 
evaluation should consider trends in actual production (increases or decreases) and 
based on this evaluation, the Division should recommend adjusting NOx intensity 
targets as necessary. 
SIP Reasonably Available Control Technology (RACT) 
Due to the reclassification to serious, Colorado must submit revisions to its SIP to 
address the Clean Air Act’s (CAA) ozone nonattainment area requirements, as set forth

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
553 
in CAA §§ 172, 182(c), and the final SIP Requirements Rule, 83 Fed. Reg. 62998 (Dec. 
6, 2018). Serious SIPs must include provisions that require the implementation of RACT 
for major sources of VOC and/or NOx (i.e., sources that emit or have the potential to 
emit 50 tpy or more) and for each category of VOC sources covered by a Control 
Technique Guideline (CTG) for which Colorado has sources in the nonattainment area. 
Therefore, to address the serious nonattainment area requirements under CAA § 
182(c), the Commission adopted revisions to Regulation Number 7 to include RACT 
requirements in Colorado’s ozone SIP for 50 tpy VOC major stationary transmission and 
storage segment compressor stations, specifically adopting a requirement for owners or 
operators of such facilities to conduct quarterly leak detection and repair inspections. 
Clean-up 
The Commission corrected the disproportionately impacted community mapping tool 
referenced in Section VII.A.7. 
Further, these revisions will include any typographical, grammatical and formatting 
errors throughout the regulation. 
Incorporation by Reference 
§ 24-4-103(12.5), C.R.S., of the State Administrative Procedure Act allows the 
Commission to incorporate by reference federal regulations. The criteria of § 24-4-
103(12.5), C.R.S., are met by including specific information and making the regulations 
available because repeating the full text of each of the federal regulations incorporated 
would be unduly cumbersome and inexpedient. To fully comply with these criteria, the 
Commission included reference dates to rules and reference methods incorporated in 
Regulation Number 7. 
Additional Considerations 
Colorado must revise Colorado’s ozone SIP to address ozone nonattainment area 
requirements. The CAA does not expressly address all of the provisions adopted by the 
Commission. Rather, federal law establishes the ozone NAAQS and requires Colorado 
to develop a SIP adequate to attain the NAAQS. Therefore, the Commission adopted 
certain revisions to Regulation Number 7 to further achieve reductions of ozone 
precursor emissions. These revisions do not exceed or differ from the federal act due to 
state flexibility in determining what control strategies to implement to reduce emissions. 
However, where the proposal may differ from federal rules under the federal act, in 
accordance with § 25-7-110.5(5)(b), C.R.S., the Commission determines 
(I) 
The revisions to Regulation Number 7 address state only requirements for drilling 
and completion operations in the oil and gas sector and SIP requirements for 
leak detection and repair at certain facilities. New Source Performance 
Standards (NSPS) OOOO, NSPS OOOOa, NSPS OOOOb, NSPS IIII, NSPS 
JJJJ, MACT HH, Maximum Achievable Control Technology (MACT) HHH, and

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
554 
MACT ZZZZ may also apply to the above listed operations and equipment. 
However, the revisions to Regulation Number 7 apply on a broader basis. 
(II) 
The federal rules discussed in (I) are primarily technology-based in that they 
largely prescribe the use of specific technologies or work practices to comply. 
The proposed state only requirements are technology- and performance- based 
that allow for maximum flexibility for demonstrating compliance. 
(III) 
The CAA establishes the 2008 and 2015 ozone NAAQS and requires Colorado 
to develop SIP revisions that will ensure attainment of the NAAQS. The ozone 
NAAQS was not determined taking into account concerns unique to Colorado. 
Similarly, EPA develops NSPS or National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air 
Pollutants (NESHAP) considering national information and data, not Colorado 
specific issues or concerns. In addition, Colorado cannot rely exclusively on a 
federally enforceable permit or federally enforceable NSPS or NESHAP to satisfy 
Colorado’s obligation to attain the ozone NAAQS. Instead, Colorado can adopt 
applicable provisions to reduce emissions of ozone precursors, as the 
Commission has done here. 
(IV) 
In addition to the 2008 ozone NAAQS, Colorado must also comply with the lower 
2015 ozone NAAQS. These current revisions may improve the ability of the 
regulated community to comply with new requirements needed to attain the lower 
NAAQS insofar as efforts conducted to support the revisions adopted by the 
Commission may prevent or reduce the need to implement additional, more 
burdensome requirements to meet the more stringent NAAQS. 
(V) 
The proposed revisions are not impacted by federal timelines.  
(VI) 
The revisions to Regulation Number 7 establish a flexible framework for 
compliance and allow for continued growth of Colorado’s industry. 
(VII) 
The revisions to Regulation Number 7 establish reasonable equity for owners 
and operators subject to these rules by providing the same standards for similarly 
situated and sized sources. 
(VIII) If Colorado does not attain the ozone NAAQS, EPA will reclassify the ozone 
control area and northern Weld County to a more stringent nonattainment area. 
This outcome would result in more stringent federal requirements for the region 
and may subject others to increased costs. 
(IX) 
Where necessary, the revisions to Regulation Number 7 include monitoring, 
recordkeeping, and reporting requirements that correlate, where possible, to 
similar federal or state requirements. 
(X) 
Demonstrated technology is available to comply with the revisions to Regulation 
Number 7. Some of the revisions expand upon requirements already applicable.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
555 
(XI) 
As set forth in the Economic Impact Analysis, the revisions to Regulation Number 
7 will reduce emissions in a cost-effective manner. 
Alternative rules could also provide reductions in ozone, VOC, and NOx to help to attain 
the NAAQS. However, a no action alternative would very likely result in failure to attain 
the ozone NAAQS. As part of adopting the revisions to Regulation Number 7, the 
Commission has taken into consideration each of the factors set forth in § 25-7-
109(1)(b), C.R.S. 
To the extent that § 25-7-110.8, C.R.S., requirements apply to this rulemaking, and after 
considering all the information in the record, the Commission hereby makes the 
determination that: 
(I) 
These rules are based upon reasonably available, validated, reviewed, and 
sound scientific methodologies, and the Commission has considered all information 
submitted by interested parties. 
(II) 
Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rules shall result in a 
demonstrable reduction of VOCs and NOx emissions. 
(III) 
Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rules shall bring about 
reductions in risks to human health and the environment that justify the costs to 
implement and comply with the rules. 
(IV) 
The rules are the most cost-effective to achieve the necessary and desired 
results, provide the regulated community flexibility, and achieve the necessary reduction 
in air pollution. 
(V) 
The rule will maximize the air quality benefits of regulation in the most cost-
effective manner.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
556 
FF. 
February 18-20, 2025 (Revisions to Part B) 
This Statement of Basis, Specific Statutory Authority, and Purpose complies with the 
requirements of the State Administrative Procedure Act, § 24-4-103(4), C.R.S., the 
Colorado Air Pollution Prevention and Control Act, §§ 25-7-110 and 25-7-110.5., C.R.S., 
and the Air Quality Control Commission’s (Commission) Procedural Rules, 5 Code 
Colo. Reg. §1001-1. 
Basis 
In March 2024, EPA finalized 40 C.F.R. Part 60, Subpart OOOOc, Emission Guidelines 
for Greenhouse Gas Emissions From Existing Crude Oil and Natural Gas Facilities (EG 
OOOOc), which triggered the requirement for states to develop plans that include 
standards of performance for all designated facilities addressed in EG OOOOc. EG 
OOOOc includes presumptive standards for storage vessels, process controllers (aka 
pneumatic controllers), pumps (aka pneumatic pumps), fugitive emissions, gas well 
liquids unloading, well associated gas, centrifugal compressors, and reciprocating 
compressors. On December 3, 2025, EPA published a final rule to extend deadlines for 
certain provisions within NSPS OOOOb , as well as the deadline for EG OOOOc state 
plan submittals by 540 days from publication. Regulation Number 7 includes 
requirements for all of these designated facilities, some of which differ from the 
presumptive standards in EG OOOOc. Therefore, the Commission adopted revisions to 
the requirements for compressors, wellhead only leak detection and repair (LDAR), 
equipment in the transmission and storage segment, and LDAR at natural gas 
processing plants, to align more closely with the presumptive standards in EG OOOOc. 
In December 2021, the Commission adopted requirements in Regulation Number 7 and 
Regulation Number 22 (the requirements in Regulation Number 22 have since been 
moved to Regulation Number 7) to address several state goals, including the 
greenhouse gas emission reduction targets for the oil and gas sector. The requirements 
include greenhouse gas intensity targets for the upstream oil and gas segment. The 
Commission revised the intensity targets for small operators to provide additional time 
for applicable operators to find technically and economically-feasible solutions to 
meeting the intensity targets.  
Further, the Commissions adopted revisions to address clarifications and typographical, 
grammatical, and formatting errors found throughout the regulation. 
Statutory Authority 
The Colorado Air Pollution Prevention and Control Act, section 25-7-101, C.R.S., et seq. 
(the State Air Act or the Act), specifically section 25-7-105(1), C.R.S., directs the 
Commission to promulgate such rules and regulations as are consistent with the 
legislative declaration set forth in section 25-7-102, C.R.S., and that are necessary for 
the proper implementation and administration of Article 7. Section 25-7-106 provides the 
Commission maximum flexibility in developing an effective air quality program and 
promulgating such a combination of regulations as may be necessary or desirable to

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
557 
carry out that program. Section 25-7-106(c) C.R.S., specifically authorizes the 
Commission to promulgate emission control regulations applicable to the entire state, 
specified areas or zones, or when a specified class of pollution is present. Sections 
109(1)(a), (2), and (3), C.R.S., authorize the Commission to promulgate regulations 
requiring effective practical air pollution controls for significant sources and categories of 
sources and emission control regulations pertaining to nitrogen oxides and 
hydrocarbons. Section 25-7-109(10), C.R.S., directs the Commission to adopt emission 
control regulations to minimize emissions of methane, other hydrocarbons, volatile 
organic compounds (VOC), and nitrogen oxides (NOx) from oil and gas operations. 
Section 25-7-106(6), C.R.S., further authorizes the Commission to require owners and 
operators of any air pollution source to monitor, record, and report information. 
Purpose 
The following section sets forth the Commission’s purpose in adopting the revisions to 
Regulation Number 7, and includes the technological and scientific rationale for the 
adoption of the revisions. 
EG OOOOc 
In March 2024, EPA finalized EG OOOOc, which triggered the requirement for states to 
develop plans that include standards of performance for all designated facilities 
addressed in EG OOOOc. With these revisions, the Commission adopted additional 
requirements for compressors, wellhead only LDAR, equipment in the transmission and 
storage segment, and LDAR at natural gas processing plants to align more closely with 
the presumptive standards in EG OOOOc. 
EG OOOOc - compressors 
Colorado has required owners or operators to reduce emissions from centrifugal 
compressor wet seal fluid degassing systems and reciprocating compressor rod packing 
since 2013, with updates in 2017 to align with recommendations in EPA’s Oil and Gas 
Control Techniques Guideline (CTG). Distinct from Colorado’s requirements, the 
presumptive standard in EPA’s EG OOOOc is for wet seal centrifugal, dry seal 
centrifugal, and reciprocating compressors to maintain seal and cylinder emissions 
below a specified volumetric flow rate. Therefore, to align with EG OOOOc, the 
Commission adopted requirements for owners or operators of subject compressors to 
comply with compressor seal and cylinder volumetric flow rates. 
Colorado has required owners or operators to collect reciprocating compressor rod 
packing VOC emissions using a rod packing emissions collection system that operates 
under negative pressure. This negative pressure requirement was also reflected in 
associated recordkeeping provisions, which required owners or operators to maintain 
records for emissions routed to a process through a closed vent system operating under 
negative pressure. In response to comments on EPA’s November 2021 proposal for 
NSPS OOOOb and EG OOOOc, however, the EPA revised the compliance alternative 
for routing rod packing emissions to a process via a closed vent system by removing the

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
558 
requirement that such routing occur under negative pressure. Commenters identified 
safety and operational concerns associated with operating under negative pressure. 
The EPA further recognized that specifying negative pressure is unnecessary because 
an adequate pressure differential is inherently created during normal compressor 
operation. Accordingly, the Commission adopted revisions to remove the negative 
pressure requirements and align Colorado’s requirements with the EPA’s updated 
approach. 
In EG OOOOc, EPA includes exemptions to the centrifugal and reciprocating 
compressor requirements for compressors located at well sites but not at centralized 
production facilities. Based on the definition of “centralized production facility” in EG 
OOOOc, and the definition of “well production facility” in Regulation Number 7, the 
Commission considers a centralized production facility to be a type and subset of well 
production facilities, similar to how wellhead only well production facilities are identified 
a type and subset of well production facilities. The Commission adopted a new definition 
for “centralized well production facility,” which explains that centralized well production 
facilities are well production facilities that gather hydrocarbon products from wells 
located at different surface sites. In the definition, the Commission clarifies that a 
separate surface site is considered to be greater than 0.25 miles from the centralized 
well production facility. A centralized well production facility can include product 
gathered from wells located at the same surface site, but must include product from 
wells gathered from other surface sites. A well production facility that gathers product 
from wells only at its own surface site is not considered a centralized well production 
facility. In Part B, Sections II.J.1. (centrifugal compressors) and II.K.1. (reciprocating 
compressors), the Commission adopted the EG OOOOc-aligning exemptions for 
compressors located at well production facilities, unless they are classified as 
centralized well production facilities specifically, pursuant to the adopted definition. This 
aligns with the exemption in EG OOOOc for compressors at well sites but not at 
centralized production facilities. However, the Commission emphasizes that adding the 
definition of centralized well production facility for purposes of the compressor 
requirements does not change other requirements applicable to centralized well 
production facilities as required for the more general term well production facilities. 
EG OOOOc - wellhead only well sites 
Colorado has required owners or operators of well production facilities to identify leaks 
from components since 2013, with updates in 2017, 2019, and 2021. Distinct from 
Colorado’s requirements, the presumptive standard in EPA’s EG OOOOc also includes 
a requirement for quarterly audio, visual, and olfactory (AVO), or any other detection 
method, inspections of single wellhead only well sites and small well sites. Colorado 
defines well production facilities differently than EPA for purposes of wellsite LDAR 
requirements. Therefore, the Commission adopted an additional wellhead only well 
production facility definition to clarify that the well production facility LDAR requirements 
also apply to wellhead only sites, to ensure that the regulation is clear that these 
facilities must be periodically inspected for leaks from components under the existing 
LDAR program. Wellhead only well production facilities are identified as a type and sub-
set of well production facilities in which the well production facility consists of only

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
559 
equipment for the wellhead; that is, the piping, casing, tubing, and valves protruding 
from the surface; and does not include other production and processing equipment that 
may be found at other well production facilities. The Commission did not adopt any new 
requirements or otherwise revise the requirements for the well production facility LDAR 
program in Part B, Section II.E. Owners and operators can conduct LDAR inspections at 
wellhead only well production facilities as part of a single permitted well production 
facility. 
During this rulemaking, the Commission heard from industry representatives that there 
may have been different interpretations over the history of the development of the LDAR 
program amongst operators regarding whether wellhead-only sites are subject to LDAR 
requirements.  The Division maintains that wellhead-only sites fall within the definition of 
well production facilities and are subject to LDAR requirements. Industry 
representatives expressed support for incorporating wellhead-only facilities into the 
LDAR program, but emphasized that operators should not be subject to enforcement for 
past non-implementation practices, given their interpretation. In retaining the existing 
LDAR program but in recognition of historic interpretations and guidance, the 
Commission understands that the Division does not intend to pursue enforcement on 
wellhead-only sites not located on a production pad, for LDAR program requirements 
prior to April 14, 2026 (i.e., effective date of this rulemaking). 
EG OOOOc - natural gas processing plants 
Colorado has required all natural gas processing plants located in the ozone 
nonattainment area to comply with LDAR requirements in a New Source Performance 
Standard (NSPS) since 2005 and statewide since 2023. The early LDAR requirements 
were to comply with NSPS KKK, which incorporates LDAR requirements from NSPS 
VV. These requirements for natural gas processing plants were updated in 2017 to 
require compliance with NSPS OOOO or NSPS OOOOa, which incorporate LDAR 
requirements from NSPS VVa, and were expanded statewide in 2021. However, EPA 
finalized a protocol for the use of optical gas imaging (OGI) in leak detection as 
appendix K to 40 CFR part 60 and, in EG OOOOc, is specifying the use of this protocol 
for application at natural gas processing plants. Therefore, the Commission adopted a 
requirement for all natural gas processing plants located in Colorado to comply with 
LDAR provisions modeled on EG OOOOc, which specifies the use of appendix K and 
an alternative use of EPA’s Method 21. In utilizing EPA’s definition of low–e packing, the 
Commission emphasizes that the definition, which ensures that materials used for valve 
repairs meet the specifications outlined, does not by itself require a drill-and-tap repair 
method. 
EG OOOOc - transmission and storage segment 
The Commission adopted a methane intensity program for the transmission and storage 
segment in 2019, which includes a requirement for owners or operators to implement 
best management practices to reduce methane emissions. In contrast, EPA’s EG 
OOOOc requires owners or operators in the transmission and storage segment to 
comply with direct requirements for storage vessels, compressors, pneumatic devices,

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
560 
and fugitive emissions from components. The Commission understands that many of 
these practices are conducted by Colorado’s transmission and storage segment owners 
or operators as best management practices. However, to further align with EG OOOOc, 
the Commission directly included these standards and work practices as direct 
requirements for the transmission and storage segment in Part B, Section IV. by way of 
referencing requirements in Part B, Sections II. and III. In using this cross-reference 
structure, the Commission does not intend to alter the applicability of Part B, Sections II. 
or III. or cause the transmission and storage segment to be independently subject to 
Part B, Sections II. or III. 
Section I. (SIP) and Section II. (111(d)) interplay 
The Commission previously adopted requirements in Part B, Sections I.G., I.J.1., I.J.2., 
and I.K. to include emission control, inspection, repair, and recordkeeping requirements 
for process unit equipment at natural gas processing plants, compressors, and 
pneumatic pumps. Those requirements pertain to VOC emissions and are included in 
Colorado’s State Implementation Plan (SIP). The new requirements for natural gas 
processing plants in Part B, Section II.I.2. and compressors in Part B, Sections II.J. and 
II.K. pertain to methane emissions and will be included in Colorado’s 111(d) plan, but 
not the SIP. Similarly, the requirements adopted in February 2025 for pneumatic pumps 
in Part B, Section III.G. pertain to methane and will be included in Colorado’s 111(d) 
plan, but not the SIP. To prevent regulatory redundancy, the Commission does not 
intend for operators to maintain compliance with the natural gas processing plant and 
compressor requirements in both Sections I. and II. or the pneumatic pump 
requirements in both Sections I. and III. Therefore, the Commission adopted revisions to 
Sections I.G.1.a., I.J.1.I., I.J.2.f., and I.K.6., which state that compliance with the natural 
gas processing plant, compressor, or pneumatic pump requirements in Sections II.I.2., 
II.J., II.K., or III.G. fulfill compliance with the natural gas processing plant, compressor, 
or pneumatic pump requirements in Section I. The Commission clarifies that while 
Section I. pertains to VOC emissions and Section II. pertains to methane emissions, 
compliance with the methane emission control requirements effectively achieves the 
necessary VOC emission controls. Operators are expected to comply only with the 
applicable Sections II. or III. requirements, which in turn fulfill compliance with the 
Section I. requirements. The Commission did not repeal the Section I. requirements, as 
those remain in Colorado’s SIP. 
Methane intensity 
The upstream greenhouse gas intensity program establishes greenhouse gas intensity 
targets for majority and minority intensity operators in the upstream oil and gas 
segment. Intensity targets are established for calendar years 2025 through 2030. The 
intensity program does not include any prescribed methods for achieving the intensity 
targets. The Commission adopted revisions that delay compliance with intensity targets 
for small operators until 2030 by removing the 2025 to 2029 intensity targets. The 
Commission did not remove the 2030 targets for these small operators. The revisions 
apply to operators with less than 45 kBOE hydrocarbon and natural gas production in 
applicable years. The purpose of these revisions is to address concerns with limited

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
561 
technically and economically-feasible options for achieving intensity targets in the short 
term for small, cost-burdened operators, particularly for carbon dioxide emissions. 
Delaying when the first intensity targets must be met by applicable operators will 
provide additional time for such operators to explore emission reduction solutions, allow 
supporting industries to respond to demands for emissions control technologies, give 
additional time for financial planning and market adjustments, allow for the marginal well 
closure funds to become fully available, and help prevent orphaned wells which could 
result in greater methane emissions. Applicable intensity operators will still be required 
to comply with the existing 2030 intensity targets, but are not required to wait until 2030 
to implement emission reduction strategies. Because the targets step down over target 
years, there may be greater emission reductions required from current levels in a 
shorter timeline to meet 2030 targets for these operators, compared to intermittently 
decreasing emissions to meet the earlier targets. The revised rule provides flexibility to 
operators who need it the most, while preserving significant emissions reductions 
through the remaining targets for minority and majority operators.      
Clean-up 
The Commission adopted additional revisions for clean-up and clarification purposes to 
provide minor enhancements to specific provisions. Adding to the definitions of “infra-
red camera” (Part B, Section II.A.18.) and “mtCO2e” (Part B, Section VIII.A.17.) 
improves the understanding of the terms. 
Further, these revisions will include corrections to any typographical, grammatical, and 
formatting errors throughout the regulation. 
Incorporation by Reference 
Section 24-4-103(12.5) of the State Administrative Procedure Act allows the 
Commission to incorporate by reference federal regulations. The criteria of § 24-4-
103(12.5) are met by including specific information and making the regulations available 
because repeating the full text of each of the federal regulations incorporated would be 
unduly cumbersome and inexpedient. To fully comply with these criteria, the 
Commission included reference dates to rules and reference methods incorporated in 
Regulation Number 7. 
Additional Considerations 
The Clean Air Act does not expressly address all of the provisions adopted by the 
Commission. Rather, federal law requires Colorado to develop a plan that includes 
standards of performance for all designated facilities addressed in an Emission 
Guideline. Therefore, the Commission adopted certain revisions to Regulation Number 
7 to reduce GHG in Colorado. 
These revisions do not exceed or differ from the federal act due to state flexibility in 
determining what control strategies to implement to reduce emissions. However, where 
the proposal may differ from federal rules under the federal act, in accordance with 
Section 25-7-110.5(5)(b), C.R.S., the Commission determines

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
562 
(I) The revisions to Regulation Number 7 address equipment and operations in the 
oil and gas sector. NSPS KKK, NSPS OOOO, NSPS OOOOa, and NSPS 
OOOOb may also apply to the above listed operations and equipment. 
However, the revisions to Regulation Number 7 apply on a broader basis. 
(II) The federal rules discussed in (I) are primarily technology-based in that they 
largely prescribe the use of specific technologies or work practices to comply. 
The proposed state only requirements are technology- and performance- 
based that allow for maximum flexibility for demonstrating compliance. 
(III)The CAA requires Colorado to develop a state plan that includes standards of 
performance for designated facilities in an Emission Guideline. EG OOOOc 
was not determined taking into account concerns unique to Colorado. EPA 
develops NSPS, Emission Guidelines, and NESHAP considering national 
information and data, not Colorado specific issues or concerns. 
(IV) 
Colorado must develop a plan that includes standards of performance for 
all designated facilities addressed in an Emission Guideline. These current 
revisions may improve the ability of the regulated community to comply with 
new requirements insofar as the revisions adopted by the Commission align, 
where possible, with existing requirements and may prevent or reduce the 
need to implement additional, more burdensome requirements. 
(V) Colorado must submit a complete state plan to EPA by January 2027 and require 
implementation of standards of performance no later than March 2029. There 
is no timing issue that might justify changing the time frame for submitting a 
state plan related to the EG OOOOc designated facilities.   
(VI) 
The revisions to Regulation Number 7 establish a flexible framework for 
compliance and allow for continued growth of Colorado’s industry. 
(VII) 
The revisions to Regulation Number 7 establish reasonable equity for 
owners and operators subject to these rules by providing the same standards 
for similarly situated and sized sources. 
(VIII) 
If Colorado does not submit a state plan that includes performance 
standards for the designated facilities in the EG OOOOc, Colorado would be 
subject to a federal plan implemented by EPA. These outcomes could result 
in more stringent federal requirements and/or requirements conflicting with 
current state requirements and may subject others to increased costs. 
(IX) 
Where necessary, the revisions to Regulation Number 7 include 
monitoring, recordkeeping, and reporting requirements that correlate, where 
possible, to similar federal or state requirements. 
(X) Demonstrated technology is available to comply with the revisions to Regulation 
Number 7. Some of the revisions expand upon requirements already 
applicable.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
563 
(XI) 
As set forth in the Economic Impact Analysis, the revisions to Regulation 
Number 7 will reduce emissions in a cost-effective manner. 
(XII) 
Alternative rules could also provide reductions in GHG to address the 
Emission Guideline. The Commission determined that the Division’s proposal 
was reasonable and cost-effective. However, a no action alternative would 
very likely result in the need for much more stringent requirements to reduce 
ozone and subject the state to a federal plan implementing the model rule 
requirements in the Emission Guideline. 
As part of adopting the revisions to Regulation Number 7, the Commission has taken 
into consideration each of the factors set forth in CRS § 25-7-109(1)(b). 
To the extent that CRS § 25-7-110.8 requirements apply to this rulemaking, and after 
considering all the information in the record, the Commission hereby makes the 
determination that: 
(I) These rules are based upon reasonably available, validated, reviewed, and 
sound scientific methodologies, and the Commission has considered all 
information submitted by interested parties. 
(II) Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rules shall result in a 
demonstrable reduction of GHG emissions. 
(III)Evidence in the record supports the finding that the rules shall bring about 
reductions in risks to human health and the environment that justify the costs 
to implement and comply with the rules. 
(IV) 
The rules are the most cost-effective to achieve the necessary and 
desired results, provide the regulated community flexibility, and achieve the 
necessary reduction in air pollution. 
(V) The rule will maximize the air quality benefits of regulation in the most cost-
effective manner.

CODE OF COLORADO REGULATIONS 
5 CCR 1001-9 
Air Quality Control Commission 
 
564
